1.ESV:
1. In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
2. The earth was without form and void, and darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters.
3. And God said, “Let there be light,” and there was light.
4. And God saw that the light was good. And God separated the light from the darkness.
5. God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And there was evening and there was morning, the first day.
6. And God said, “Let there be an expanse in the midst of the waters, and let it separate the waters from the waters.”
7. And God made the expanse and separated the waters that were under the expanse from the waters that were above the expanse. And it was so.
8. And God called the expanse Heaven. And there was evening and there was morning, the second day.
9. And God said, “Let the waters under the heavens be gathered together into one place, and let the dry land appear.” And it was so.
10. God called the dry land Earth, and the waters that were gathered together he called Seas. And God saw that it was good.
11. And God said, “Let the earth sprout vegetation, plants yielding seed, and fruit trees bearing fruit in which is their seed, each according to its kind, on the earth.” And it was so.
12. The earth brought forth vegetation, plants yielding seed according to their own kinds, and trees bearing fruit in which is their seed, each according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.
13. And there was evening and there was morning, the third day.
14. And God said, “Let there be lights in the expanse of the heavens to separate the day from the night. And let them be for signs and for seasons, and for days and years,
15. and let them be lights in the expanse of the heavens to give light upon the earth.” And it was so.
16. And God made the two great lights—the greater light to rule the day and the lesser light to rule the night—and the stars.
17. And God set them in the expanse of the heavens to give light on the earth,
18. to rule over the day and over the night, and to separate the light from the darkness. And God saw that it was good.
19. And there was evening and there was morning, the fourth day.
20. And God said, “Let the waters swarm with swarms of living creatures, and let birds fly above the earth across the expanse of the heavens.”
21. So God created the great sea creatures and every living creature that moves, with which the waters swarm, according to their kinds, and every winged bird according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.
22. And God blessed them, saying, “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the waters in the seas, and let birds multiply on the earth.”
23. And there was evening and there was morning, the fifth day.
24. And God said, “Let the earth bring forth living creatures according to their kinds—livestock and creeping things and beasts of the earth according to their kinds.” And it was so.
25. And God made the beasts of the earth according to their kinds and the livestock according to their kinds, and everything that creeps on the ground according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.
26. Then God said, “Let us make man in our image, after our likeness. And let them have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over the livestock and over all the earth and over every creeping thing that creeps on the earth.”
27. So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.
28. And God blessed them. And God said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing that moves on the earth.”
29. And God said, “Behold, I have given you every plant yielding seed that is on the face of all the earth, and every tree with seed in its fruit. You shall have them for food.
30. And to every beast of the earth and to every bird of the heavens and to everything that creeps on the earth, everything that has the breath of life, I have given every green plant for food.” And it was so.
31. And God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good. And there was evening and there was morning, the sixth day.

1.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God creates the universe in six days by His word, culminating in humanity made in His image.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses. Jewish and Christian tradition unanimously attribute Genesis
to Moses (cf. Exodus 17:14, John 5:46-47, Luke 24:27, 44). Written
approximately 1445-1405 BC during the wilderness wanderings.

Title: "Genesis" = Greek for "origin/beginning." Hebrew title: "Bereshith" =
"In the beginning." The book of beginnings — the origin of the universe,
life, humanity, sin, death, nations, languages, and redemption.

Purpose: To reveal God as Creator, to establish humanity's identity and
purpose, and to begin the story of redemption that culminates in Christ.
Genesis answers the fundamental questions: Where did we come from? Why are we
here? What went wrong? What is God doing about it?

Chapter 1 Overview: The creation account — six days of God creating the
universe by His word, culminating in humanity made in His image, followed by
the seventh day of rest (2:1-3). The chapter establishes God's sovereignty,
power, wisdom, and goodness. The repeated refrain: "And God said... and it
was so... and God saw that it was good."

Structure of the Six Days:
- Days 1-3: Forming (giving shape to the formless)
  Day 1: Light/darkness. Day 2: Sky/waters. Day 3: Land/seas/vegetation.
- Days 4-6: Filling (populating the void)
  Day 4: Sun/moon/stars. Day 5: Sea creatures/birds. Day 6: Animals/humanity.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Eden's exact location is debated, but the Tigris and Euphrates rivers
  (modern Iraq) are named in 2:10-14, placing the setting in ancient
  Mesopotamia.
• The creation account presents the entire earth as God's domain — no single
  location is emphasized because the scope is cosmic and universal.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | John        | 1       | 1-3                | "In the beginning was the Word." (ESV)
v.1              | Hebrews     | 11      | 3                  | "By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God." (ESV)
v.2              | Psalm       | 104     | 30                 | "When you send forth your Spirit, they are created." (ESV)
v.3              | 2 Corinthians | 4     | 6                  | "God, who said, 'Let light shine out of darkness.'" (ESV)
v.26             | Psalm       | 8       | 5-6                | "You have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings and crowned him with glory." (ESV)
v.27             | Matthew     | 19      | 4                  | "He who created them from the beginning made them male and female." (ESV)
v.28             | Psalm       | 115     | 16                 | "The heavens are the LORD's heavens, but the earth he has given to the children of man." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-1/
  Guzik covers the creation account, the meaning of "created," the six days,
  the image of God, and the cultural mandate.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "In the Beginning" on v.1 (MTP Vol. 34, No. 2028)
  "In the beginning GOD — not matter, not chance, not evolution, not nothing
  — God. Before anything else existed, God was. He is the uncaused cause, the
  unmade maker, the uncreated creator. And He CREATED — brought into existence
  from nothing. Not shaped pre-existing material but spoke into being what
  had no being. This is the foundation of all theology: God is, and God
  creates."
  Sermon: "The Image of God" on v.27 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 619)
  "In the image of God created he him — not in the image of animals, not
  evolved from beasts, but made in God's image. What does this mean? Not
  physical resemblance but moral likeness — rationality, personality,
  creativity, moral awareness, relational capacity. You bear God's stamp.
  However fallen, however marred — the image remains. Every human being
  carries divine dignity."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "the heaven" / ESV: "the heavens" — Hebrew "hashamayim" — the Hebrew is plural ("shamayim"), so ESV's "heavens" is more literal. KJV uses singular "heaven" as a collective. Both convey the same meaning: the entire expanse above.
v.1              | KJV         | "In the beginning" / ESV: "In the beginning" — Both identical. Hebrew "bereshith" — in the beginning of, at the start of. The absolute beginning of created time and space. "God" — Hebrew "Elohim" — plural form (more than two) with singular verb ("created" = "bara" singular). Plurality in unity — early hint of the Trinity.
v.1              | KJV         | "created" / ESV: "created" — Both identical. Hebrew "bara" — to create from nothing (ex nihilo). Used only of God in the OT. Distinguished from "asah" (to make/form from existing material) and "yatsar" (to shape/fashion).
v.2              | KJV         | "without form, and void" / ESV: "without form and void" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "tohu wabohu" — formless and empty. Not chaotic evil but unformed and unfilled — ready to be shaped and populated in the six days.
v.2              | KJV         | "moved upon the face of the waters" / ESV: "was hovering over the face of the waters" — Hebrew "merachephet" — hovered, brooded (like a bird over its nest). ESV's "hovering" captures the Hebrew participle more precisely than KJV's "moved." The Spirit actively present, nurturing creation.
v.5              | KJV         | "the evening and the morning were the first day" / ESV: "there was evening and there was morning, the first day" — Hebrew days begin at sunset. ESV renders the Hebrew construction more literally ("there was evening and there was morning"). Both convey the same meaning: each day = evening (darkness) then morning (light) = one complete day.
v.6              | KJV         | "firmament" / ESV: "expanse" — Hebrew "raqia" — something spread out or stretched. KJV's "firmament" (from Latin Vulgate "firmamentum") can imply a solid dome; ESV's "expanse" better conveys the Hebrew sense of an open, stretched-out space.
v.11             | KJV         | "bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed" / ESV: "sprout vegetation, plants yielding seed" — Hebrew "deshe" (vegetation/grass) and "eseb" (herb/plant). ESV uses "sprout" for the verb (more vivid) and "vegetation" / "plants" for modern clarity. KJV's "grass" and "herb" are older English equivalents.
v.20             | KJV         | "bring forth abundantly the moving creature that hath life, and fowl" / ESV: "swarm with swarms of living creatures, and let birds fly" — Hebrew "yishretsu sherets" (swarm with swarms) and "oph" (birds/fowl). ESV captures the Hebrew wordplay of "swarm with swarms" and uses "birds" instead of archaic "fowl."
v.21             | KJV         | "great whales" / ESV: "great sea creatures" — Hebrew "tanninim gedolim" — large sea creatures. ESV's "sea creatures" is broader and more accurate; "tanninim" refers to large marine animals generally, not specifically whales.
v.26             | KJV         | "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness" / ESV: "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness" — Both identical. Hebrew "na'aseh" — first person plural. God speaks within Himself — the Trinity in consultation. "Image" (tselem) = representation, likeness. "Likeness" (demuth) = resemblance, similarity.
v.27             | KJV         | "male and female created he them" / ESV: "male and female he created them" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "zakar unqebah" — both genders equally bear God's image. Neither is more "image" than the other. Minor word order difference only.
v.28             | KJV         | "replenish the earth" / ESV: "fill the earth" — Hebrew "mil'u" — to fill. ESV's "fill" is more accurate to the Hebrew. KJV's "replenish" in 1611 English meant "fill" (not "refill"), but modern readers may misunderstand it as implying a previous filling.
v.29             | KJV         | "every herb bearing seed... to you it shall be for meat" / ESV: "every plant yielding seed... You shall have them for food" — Hebrew "oklah" = food. KJV's "meat" in 1611 English meant "food" generally, not specifically animal flesh. ESV's "food" avoids confusion.
v.31             | KJV         | "very good" / ESV: "very good" — Both identical. Hebrew "tob meod" — exceedingly good. The only day rated "very good" — because humanity completes creation.
v.2              | ASV         | "waste and void" — more stark than KJV/ESV's "without form and void." The word "waste" emphasizes desolation, capturing the Hebrew "tohu" as barren wasteland rather than mere shapelessness.
v.2              | NET         | "without shape and empty...the watery deep" — NET uniquely renders "tehom" as "the watery deep" (rather than simply "the deep"), emphasizing the aquatic nature of the primordial chaos.
v.5              | NET         | "marking the first day" — adds interpretive clarity that evening and morning together "mark" a complete day, making the day-structure explicit where KJV/ESV leave it implicit.
v.21             | ASV         | "great sea-monsters" — more vivid than KJV's "great whales" or ESV's "great sea creatures." Translates Hebrew "tanninim" with a term that captures the mythic resonance of the original.
v.26             | NET         | "Let us make humankind" — uses gender-inclusive "humankind" rather than "man" (KJV/ESV/ASV), reflecting the collective meaning of Hebrew "adam" in this context (male and female together, v.27).

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Elohim            | The Hebrew name for God used throughout Genesis 1. Plural in form, singular in meaning. Emphasizes God's power and majesty. Hints at plurality within the Godhead. | 
Bara              | To create from nothing. Used exclusively of God. Distinct from making or forming from existing material. | 
Tohu wabohu       | "Without form and void." The initial state of creation before God shaped and filled it over six days. Image of God (Imago Dei) — Humanity's unique status as God's representatives. Includes rationality, morality, creativity, relationality, and dominion. | 
Dominion          | Humanity's God-given authority to rule over creation as God's stewards. Not exploitation but responsible care. Cultural mandate — God's command to be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth, and subdue it. The call to develop creation's potential. "And God said" — Creation by divine speech. God's word has creative power. He speaks and reality responds. "It was good"  — God's evaluation of His creation. Everything He made was good — functioning as intended, reflecting His character. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "In the beginning God" (v.1). Everything starts with God. Is He the
   starting point of your thinking, your decisions, your worldview? Or do
   you start with yourself and fit God in later?

2. "God said... and it was so" (vv.3, 6, 9, etc.). God's word has creative
   power. When He speaks, reality responds. Do you treat His word
   (Scripture) with that kind of authority in your life?

3. "Let us make man in our image" (v.26). You are made in God's image —
   designed to reflect Him. How does this shape your sense of identity,
   purpose, and worth? How does it shape how you treat others?

4. "Be fruitful, and multiply... and have dominion" (v.28). God gave
   humanity a mission: fill the earth and steward it well. How are you
   fulfilling this mandate — in your family, your work, your community?

5. "God saw every thing... and, behold, it was very good" (v.31). Creation
   was originally good — not evil, not meaningless. Sin corrupted it, but
   goodness is the original design. How does this shape your view of the
   physical world, your body, and material creation?

6. The chapter shows God as powerful (He creates by speaking), wise
   (creation is ordered and purposeful), and good (everything is "good").
   Which attribute of God do you most need to trust today?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-1/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

2.ESV:
1. Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them.
2. And on the seventh day God finished his work that he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work that he had done.
3. So God blessed the seventh day and made it holy, because on it God rested from all his work that he had done in creation.
4. These are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens.
5. When no bush of the field was yet in the land and no small plant of the field had yet sprung up—for the LORD God had not caused it to rain on the land, and there was no man to work the ground,
6. and a mist was going up from the land and was watering the whole face of the ground—
7. then the LORD God formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature.
8. And the LORD God planted a garden in Eden, in the east, and there he put the man whom he had formed.
9. And out of the ground the LORD God made to spring up every tree that is pleasant to the sight and good for food. The tree of life was in the midst of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
10. A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and there it divided and became four rivers.
11. The name of the first is the Pishon. It is the one that flowed around the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.
12. And the gold of that land is good; bdellium and onyx stone are there.
13. The name of the second river is the Gihon. It is the one that flowed around the whole land of Cush.

2.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God forms man from dust, breathes life into him, plants Eden, and creates woman from man's side as his perfect complement.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 2 is not a second creation account but a detailed
expansion of Day 6 — focusing on the creation of man and woman and the
establishment of Eden. Chapter 1 gives the cosmic overview; chapter 2 zooms
in on humanity. The name "LORD God" (Yahweh Elohim) appears for the first
time — combining God's covenant name (personal, relational) with His power
name (majestic, sovereign).

God's Rest (vv.1-3): God finished His work and rested on the seventh day
(v.2). He blessed and sanctified it (v.3). Not rest from exhaustion but rest
of completion — the work is done. This establishes the Sabbath pattern.

Man's Creation (vv.4-7): God "formed man of the dust of the ground" (v.7) —
hands-on, intimate creation. Then "breathed into his nostrils the breath of
life" (v.7). Man is both earthly (dust) and divine (God's breath). A "living
soul" — body + breath = life.

The Garden of Eden (vv.8-17): God planted a garden — paradise (v.8). Two
special trees: the tree of life and the tree of knowledge of good and evil
(v.9). One command: eat freely of everything EXCEPT the tree of knowledge —
"in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die" (vv.16-17).
Freedom with one boundary.

The Creation of Woman (vv.18-25): "It is not good that the man should be
alone" (v.18) — the first "not good" in creation. No animal was a suitable
partner (vv.19-20). God made woman from man's rib — "bone of my bones, and
flesh of my flesh" (v.23). Marriage established: leave, cleave, one flesh
(v.24). Naked without shame (v.25).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Eden is described with four rivers: Pishon, Gihon, Tigris (Hiddekel), and
  Euphrates (vv.10-14), placing it in the Mesopotamian region (modern Iraq).
• The lands of Havilah (possibly Arabia) and Cush (possibly Ethiopia or
  Mesopotamian Kush) are mentioned in connection with the rivers.
• The garden functions as sacred space — a temple-like place where God dwells
  with humanity. Geography here is theological as much as physical.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Hebrews     | 4       | 4,10               | "God rested on the seventh day from all his works." (ESV)
v.7              | 1 Corinthians | 15    | 45                 | "The first man Adam became a living being." (ESV)
v.17             | Romans      | 6       | 23                 | "The wages of sin is death." (ESV)
v.18             | Ecclesiastes | 4      | 9-10               | "Two are better than one." (ESV)
v.21-22          | Ephesians   | 5       | 30-31              | "We are members of his body." (ESV)
v.24             | Matthew     | 19      | 5                  | "A man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife." (ESV)
v.24             | Ephesians   | 5       | 31                 | "The two shall become one flesh." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-2/
  Guzik covers the Sabbath rest, man's formation, Eden, the prohibition,
  and the creation of woman and marriage.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The First Man Adam" on v.7 (MTP Vol. 15, No. 888)
  "Formed of the dust — how humble our origin! Breathed into by God — how
  exalted our dignity! We are dust animated by divinity. Earthly in substance
  but heavenly in life. This is man's paradox: made from the lowest material
  but given the highest gift — the breath of God Himself."
  Sermon: "The Marriage Bond" on v.24 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1316)
  "Leave — separation from the old. Cleave — attachment to the new. One flesh
  — union that cannot be divided. God designed marriage before sin entered,
  before the fall corrupted. It is not a human invention but a divine
  institution — established in paradise by God Himself."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.2              | KJV         | "ended his work" / ESV: "finished his work" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "wayekal" — completed, finished. KJV's "ended" and ESV's "finished" convey the same meaning. ceased, stopped, desisted. Root of "Sabbath." Not exhaustion but completion. God stopped because the work was finished.
v.3              | KJV         | "sanctified it" / ESV: "made it holy" — Hebrew "wayeqaddesh" — set apart, made holy. ESV renders the Hebrew verb more transparently for modern readers; KJV uses the Latinate "sanctified." Same meaning.
v.4              | KJV         | "LORD God" / ESV: "LORD God" — Both identical. Hebrew "Yahweh Elohim." First use of God's covenant name (Yahweh/LORD) combined with His power name (Elohim/God). Personal + powerful. Relational + sovereign.
v.5              | KJV         | "every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew" / ESV: "When no bush of the field was yet in the land and no small plant of the field had yet sprung up" — Hebrew "siach" (bush/shrub) and "eseb" (plant/herb). ESV restructures the sentence for clarity and uses "bush" and "small plant" for more precise rendering.
v.6              | KJV         | "a mist" / ESV: "a mist" — Both identical. Hebrew "ed" — mist, vapor, or spring. The exact meaning is debated; both translations use "mist."
v.7              | KJV         | "formed man of the dust of the ground" / ESV: "formed the man of dust from the ground" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "yatsar" — to shape, mold, fashion (like a potter with clay). Intimate, hands-on creation. chayyah" — a living being, a breathing creature. ESV's "creature" avoids the theological baggage modern readers attach to "soul" (the same Hebrew phrase is used of animals in 1:24). KJV's "soul" reflects older English usage meaning "living being."
v.13             | KJV         | "Ethiopia" / ESV: "Cush" — Hebrew "Kush." ESV retains the Hebrew name; KJV uses the Greek/English equivalent. Cush refers to the region south of Egypt (modern Sudan/Ethiopia area).
v.14             | KJV         | "Hiddekel" / ESV: "Tigris" — Hebrew "Chiddeqel." ESV uses the modern name (Tigris) for clarity; KJV transliterates the Hebrew.
v.15             | KJV         | "to dress it and to keep it" / ESV: "to work it and keep it" — Hebrew "le'abdah uleshomrah" — to serve/work it and to guard/keep it. ESV's "work" is more natural modern English than KJV's "dress" (which meant "tend" in 1611).
v.17             | KJV         | "thou shalt surely die" / ESV: "you shall surely die" — Both equivalent (difference is only thou/you). Hebrew "moth tamuth" — dying you shall die. An emphatic construction: death is certain. Spiritual death immediately; physical death eventually.
v.18             | KJV         | "an help meet for him" / ESV: "a helper fit for him" — Hebrew "ezer kenegdo" — a helper corresponding to him, a counterpart suitable for him. KJV's "help meet" is two words (help + meet/ suitable); ESV's "helper fit" captures the same idea more clearly. "Ezer" is used of God Himself (Psalm 33:20, 70:5) — not a subordinate term but a strong one.
v.19             | KJV         | "every fowl of the air" / ESV: "every bird of the heavens" — Hebrew "oph hashamayim." ESV uses "bird" (modern) vs. KJV's "fowl" (archaic), and "heavens" (literal Hebrew) vs. "air."
v.21             | KJV         | "rib" / ESV: "rib" — Both identical. Hebrew "tsela" — side, rib, or side-chamber. Woman taken from man's side — not his head (to rule over him) or his feet (to be trampled) but his side (to be equal, near his heart).
v.23             | KJV         | "This is now bone of my bones" / ESV: "This at last is bone of my bones" — Hebrew "zoth happa'am" — this time, now, at last. ESV's "at last" captures the sense of joyful culmination after naming all the animals and finding no match.
v.24             | KJV         | "cleave unto his wife" / ESV: "hold fast to his wife" — Hebrew "dabaq" — to cling, stick to, be joined permanently. The strongest word for attachment. ESV's "hold fast" is more accessible to modern readers than KJV's "cleave" (which now commonly means "to split"). TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------ - WEB uses "Yahweh" throughout for the divine name (KJV/ESV: "the LORD"). This reflects the actual pronunciation of the Hebrew Tetragrammaton YHWH. ASV uses "Jehovah" — an older English hybrid form of the same name.
v.6              | NET         | "Springs would well up from the earth" — renders Hebrew "ed" as "springs" rather than "mist" (KJV/ESV/ASV/WEB), reflecting a scholarly view that the term refers to subterranean water sources, not atmospheric vapor.
v.8              | NET         | "planted an orchard" — uniquely renders Hebrew "gan" as "orchard" rather than "garden" (KJV/ESV/ASV/WEB), emphasizing the food-producing trees.
v.18             | NET         | "a companion for him who corresponds to him" — interprets "ezer kenegdo" with relational language ("companion") vs. functional ("helper").
v.21             | NET         | "took part of the man's side" — avoids committing to "rib," reflecting the broader meaning of Hebrew "tsela" (side/side-chamber).
v.24             | NET         | "they become one family" — a distinctive rendering where all others have "one flesh," interpreting the unity as household/kinship.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Sabbath/Rest      | God's cessation from creative work on the seventh day. Not exhaustion but completion. The pattern for human rest. Formed from dust — Humanity's humble material origin. We are earthly, physical, mortal — apart from God's breath. Breath of life — God's direct, personal gift of life to humanity. What distinguishes humans from mere matter. | 
Tree of life      | Access to eternal life in God's presence. Lost at the fall; restored in Revelation 22. Tree of knowledge — The one prohibition. Not evil in itself but the test of obedience. The boundary of human freedom. Help meet/Ezer — A helper corresponding to the man. A strong term (used of God) meaning a counterpart, a complement, an equal partner. | 
One flesh         | The profound union of marriage — physical, emotional, spiritual. Two becoming one. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "God rested on the seventh day" (v.2). God modeled rest. Do you rest? Or
   do you work endlessly, as if everything depends on you? Rest is not
   laziness — it's trust.

2. "Formed man of the dust... breathed into his nostrils the breath of life"
   (v.7). You are both humble (dust) and dignified (God's breath). Do you
   live in both realities — humble before God, yet confident in your worth?

3. "Of every tree... thou mayest freely eat: but..." (vv.16-17). God gave
   enormous freedom with one boundary. How do you respond to God's
   boundaries — with gratitude for the freedom or resentment at the
   restriction?

4. "It is not good that the man should be alone" (v.18). God designed us for
   relationship. Are you investing in deep, meaningful relationships? Or
   trying to live in isolation?

5. "Leave... cleave... one flesh" (v.24). If you're married, are you fully
   "leaving" (old attachments) and "cleaving" (new commitment)? Is your
   marriage reflecting God's design?

3. but God said, ‘You shall not eat of the fruit of the tree that is in the midst of the garden, neither shall you touch it, lest you die.’”
4. But the serpent said to the woman, “You will not surely die.
5. For God knows that when you eat of it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.”

3.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The serpent tempts Eve, Adam and Eve eat the forbidden fruit, sin and death enter creation, but God promises a Redeemer.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 3 is the most consequential chapter in the Bible
after Genesis 1. It records the fall of humanity — the entrance of sin, death,
and curse into God's good creation. Everything that follows in Scripture (and
in human history) is the result of what happens here. But even in judgment,
God provides hope: the first gospel promise (v.15) and the first sacrifice
(v.21). The chapter answers the question: What went wrong?

The Temptation (vv.1-6): The serpent (Satan, cf. Rev 12:9) questions God's
word: "Yea, hath God said?" (v.1). He denies God's warning: "Ye shall not
surely die" (v.4). He impugns God's motive: "God doth know..." (v.5). Eve
sees three appeals: good for food (flesh), pleasant to eyes (eyes), desired
for wisdom (pride) — cf. 1 John 2:16. She eats and gives to Adam (v.6).

The Consequences (vv.7-13): Eyes opened — shame, nakedness, fig leaves (v.7).
They hide from God (v.8). God seeks them: "Where art thou?" (v.9). Adam
blames Eve; Eve blames the serpent (vv.12-13).

The Judgments (vv.14-19): Serpent cursed — on its belly, enmity with woman's
seed (vv.14-15). The protoevangelium (v.15): her seed will crush the
serpent's head. Woman: pain in childbirth, relational conflict (v.16). Man:
cursed ground, toil, sweat, death — "dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou
return" (vv.17-19).

Grace in Judgment (vv.20-24): Adam names Eve "mother of all living" — faith
in God's promise of life (v.20). God makes coats of skins — the first death,
the first blood sacrifice, the first covering (v.21). Expelled from Eden —
but mercifully, to prevent eternal life in a fallen state (vv.22-24).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Eden — humanity is expelled eastward (v.24). In Genesis, moving "east"
  consistently symbolizes movement away from God's presence (cf. 4:16, 11:2).
• Cherubim and a flaming sword guard the eastern entrance to Eden (v.24),
  indicating Eden as sacred space with controlled access — a proto-temple.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Revelation  | 12      | 9                  | "That ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan." (ESV)
v.6              | 1 John      | 2       | 16                 | "The desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life." (ESV)
v.7              | Isaiah      | 61      | 10                 | "He has clothed me with the garments of salvation." (ESV)
v.15             | Romans      | 16      | 20                 | "The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet." (ESV)
v.15             | Galatians   | 4       | 4                  | "God sent forth his Son, born of woman." (ESV)
v.17-19          | Romans      | 8       | 20-22              | "The creation was subjected to futility." (ESV)
v.21             | Hebrews     | 9       | 22                 | "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins." (ESV)
v.24             | Revelation  | 22      | 14                 | "That they may have the right to the tree of life." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-3/
  Guzik covers the serpent's strategy, the fall, the consequences, the
  protoevangelium, and God's grace in judgment.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Protoevangelium" on v.15 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 829)
  "It shall bruise thy head — a fatal wound. Thou shalt bruise his heel — a
  painful but not fatal wound. Here is the gospel in seed form: the woman's
  seed (Christ, born of a virgin) will crush the serpent's head (destroy
  Satan's power) at the cost of His own suffering (the cross — a bruised
  heel). The first promise of redemption — spoken not to Adam but to the
  serpent, in Adam's hearing. Grace announced in the very moment of judgment."
  Sermon: "Where Art Thou?" on v.9 (MTP Vol. 5, No. 264)
  "Where art thou? — not because God didn't know but because Adam needed to
  face his condition. God seeks the sinner. The sinner hides; God pursues.
  Adam ran; God called. This is the pattern of all redemption: God seeking
  those who are hiding from Him. He is the seeking God — and He has never
  stopped calling."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "subtil" / ESV: "crafty" — Hebrew "arum" — crafty, shrewd, cunning. ESV's "crafty" is more immediately understood by modern readers than KJV's archaic "subtil." The same root as "naked" (arom) in 2:25 — a wordplay connecting the chapters.
v.1              | KJV         | "Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree?" / ESV: "Did God actually say, 'You shall not eat of any tree in the garden'?" — Both convey the same distortion. ESV's "actually" captures the incredulous tone of the Hebrew. Satan's first recorded words: a question that casts doubt on God's word.
v.5              | KJV         | "ye shall be as gods" / ESV: "you will be like God" — Hebrew "kelohim" — like God/gods. The Hebrew "Elohim" can be translated either way. ESV takes it as referring to God Himself; KJV's "gods" (lowercase) suggests becoming divine beings. Both are legitimate readings; ESV's is more common among modern scholars.
v.6              | KJV         | "pleasant to the eyes" / ESV: "a delight to the eyes" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "ta'avah" — desirable, delightful. Three categories matching 1 John 2:16: lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, pride of life.
v.7              | KJV         | "made themselves aprons" / ESV: "made themselves loincloths" — Hebrew "chagoroth" — coverings for the waist/loins. ESV's "loincloths" is more precise; KJV's "aprons" reflects 1611 English usage for a waist-covering garment.
v.8              | KJV         | "the voice of the LORD God walking" / ESV: "the sound of the LORD God walking" — Hebrew "qol" — voice, sound, noise. ESV's "sound" is more natural here (one hears the sound of someone walking); KJV's "voice" is a more literal default for "qol."
v.15             | KJV         | "it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel" / Hebrew "hu yeshupeka rosh" — he (masculine pronoun) shall crush your head. ESV correctly renders the masculine pronoun "he" (pointing to a specific male descendant — Christ). KJV's "it" obscures the personal reference. "Bruise" — Hebrew "shuph" — crush, strike. Head-crushing = fatal; heel-bruising = painful but survivable.
v.16             | KJV         | "I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception" / ESV: "I will surely multiply your pain in childbearing" — Hebrew "itstsabon" — pain, toil, labor. ESV's "pain in childbearing" is clearer than KJV's "sorrow and thy conception" which can confuse modern readers.
v.17             | KJV         | "cursed is the ground for thy sake" / ESV: "cursed is the ground because of you" — Hebrew "ba'abureka" — on your account, because of you. ESV is more transparent; KJV's "for thy sake" could be misread as "for your benefit."
v.19             | KJV         | "In the sweat of thy face" / ESV: "By the sweat of your face" — Both equivalent. Minor preposition difference only.
v.21             | KJV         | "coats of skins" / ESV: "garments of skins" — Hebrew "kotnoth or" — tunics/garments of skin. Both equivalent. An animal died to provide covering. The first death in Scripture. The first blood shed. The first substitution. God Himself provides the covering — humanity's fig leaves are inadequate.
v.24             | KJV         | "Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way" / ESV: "the cherubim and a flaming sword that turned every way to guard the way" — Hebrew "lishmor" — to guard, keep, watch over. ESV's "guard" is more specific than KJV's "keep." Also, KJV adds English plural "-s" to "Cherubim" (already plural in Hebrew); ESV correctly uses "cherubim" without the extra "s." TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | NET         | "shrewder than any of the wild animals" — uses "shrewder" (morally neutral term for intelligence) rather than "subtle/crafty" (KJV/ESV), and "wild animals" instead of "beasts of the field."
v.8              | NET         | "the breezy time of the day" — a distinctive rendering of Hebrew "ruach hayom" (KJV/ESV: "cool of the day"), emphasizing the wind/ breeze element of "ruach" rather than temperature.
v.15             | WEB         | "hostility between you and the woman...He will bruise your head" — uses "hostility" (also NET) rather than "enmity" (KJV/ESV), more accessible to modern readers. Correctly renders masculine "He."
v.16             | NET         | "You will want to control your husband, but he will dominate you" — a strikingly interpretive rendering. KJV/ESV: "thy desire shall be to thy husband." NET makes explicit the conflict interpretation of Hebrew "teshuqah" (desire/longing as desire to control, cf. Gen 4:7).
v.24             | NET         | "angelic sentries who used the flame of a whirling sword" — expands "cherubim" to "angelic sentries" for clarity and renders the Hebrew construction as an instrumental use ("used the flame"), making the image more vivid than KJV/ESV's more literal rendering.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The serpent       | Satan using a serpent as his instrument. Crafty, deceptive, questioning God's word and character. "Hath God said?" — Satan's primary strategy: casting doubt on God's word. If he can make you question Scripture, he can make you fall. | 
The fall          | Humanity's rebellion against God through disobedience. The entrance of sin, death, and curse into creation. Protoevangelium — "First gospel." Genesis 3:15 — the first promise of a Redeemer who will crush Satan at the cost of His own suffering. Seed of the woman — Christ. Born of a woman (virgin birth implied — seed of woman, not man). The one who crushes the serpent's head. Coats of skins — God's provision of covering through animal sacrifice. The first picture of substitutionary atonement. | 
Curse             | The consequences of sin on the serpent, the woman, the man, and the ground. Reversed ultimately in Christ (Rev 22:3). "Where art thou?" — God seeking fallen humanity. Not ignorance but invitation. God pursues those who hide from Him. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Yea, hath God said?" (v.1). Where is Satan causing you to doubt God's
   word? His strategy hasn't changed: question what God said, deny the
   consequences, impugn God's motives. How do you respond?

2. "She took... and did eat, and gave also unto her husband" (v.6). Sin
   rarely stays private — it spreads. Where might your sin be affecting
   others?

3. "Where art thou?" (v.9). God seeks you even when you're hiding. Are you
   hiding from God right now — in shame, guilt, or fear? He's calling. Will
   you answer?

4. "It shall bruise thy head" (v.15). Even in the darkest moment of human
   history, God promises redemption. The Redeemer is coming. Whatever
   darkness you face, God has already planned the victory.

5. "The LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them" (v.21). Your fig
   leaves (self-effort, self-righteousness) are inadequate. Only God can
   provide proper covering — and it costs a life. Have you received His
   covering?

4.ESV:
1. Now Adam knew Eve his wife, and she conceived and bore Cain, saying, “I have gotten a man with the help of the LORD.”
2. And again, she bore his brother Abel. Now Abel was a keeper of sheep, and Cain a worker of the ground.
3. In the course of time Cain brought to the LORD an offering of the fruit of the ground,
4. and Abel also brought of the firstborn of his flock and of their fat portions. And the LORD had regard for Abel and his offering,
5. but for Cain and his offering he had no regard. So Cain was very angry, and his face fell.
6. The LORD said to Cain, “Why are you angry, and why has your face fallen?
7. If you do well, will you not be accepted? And if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you, and you must rule over it.”
8. Cain spoke to Abel his brother. And when they were in the field, Cain rose up against his brother Abel and killed him.
9. Then the LORD said to Cain, “Where is Abel your brother?” He said, “I do not know; am I my brother’s keeper?”
10. And the LORD said, “What have you done? The voice of your brother’s blood is crying to me from the ground.
11. And now you are cursed from the ground, which has opened its mouth to receive your brother’s blood from your hand.
12. When you work the ground, it shall no longer yield to you its strength. You shall be a fugitive and a wanderer on the earth.”
13. Cain said to the LORD, “My punishment is greater than I can bear.
14. Behold, you have driven me today away from the ground, and from your face I shall be hidden. I shall be a fugitive and a wanderer on the earth, and whoever finds me will kill me.”
15. Then the LORD said to him, “Not so! If anyone kills Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold.” And the LORD put a mark on Cain, lest any who found him should attack him.
16. Then Cain went away from the presence of the LORD and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.
17. Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bore Enoch. When he built a city, he called the name of the city after the name of his son, Enoch.
18. To Enoch was born Irad, and Irad fathered Mehujael, and Mehujael fathered Methushael, and Methushael fathered Lamech.
19. And Lamech took two wives. The name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah.
20. Adah bore Jabal; he was the father of those who dwell in tents and have livestock.
21. His brother’s name was Jubal; he was the father of all those who play the lyre and pipe.
22. Zillah also bore Tubal-cain; he was the forger of all instruments of bronze and iron. The sister of Tubal-cain was Naamah.
23. Lamech said to his wives: “Adah and Zillah, hear my voice; you wives of Lamech, listen to what I say: I have killed a man for wounding me, a young man for striking me.
24. If Cain’s revenge is sevenfold, then Lamech’s is seventy-sevenfold.”
25. And Adam knew his wife again, and she bore a son and called his name Seth, for she said, “God has appointed for me another offspring instead of Abel, for Cain killed him.”
26. To Seth also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. At that time people began to call upon the name of the LORD.

4.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Cain murders Abel out of jealousy over God's acceptance of Abel's sacrifice; sin escalates rapidly in the first family.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 shows the immediate consequences of the fall —
sin spreading from the first couple to the first family. Cain murders Abel
in the first act of human violence, demonstrating that sin escalates rapidly.
The chapter traces two lines: Cain's line (vv.17-24) — characterized by
civilization without God, ending in Lamech's boastful violence — and Seth's
line (vv.25-26) — the line of faith through which the Redeemer will come.

Cain and Abel's Offerings (vv.1-7): Both bring offerings to God. Abel brings
"the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof" — the best, with blood
(v.4). Cain brings "of the fruit of the ground" — no indication of firstfruits
or best (v.3). God accepts Abel's; rejects Cain's (vv.4-5). The difference:
not the type of offering but the heart behind it (cf. Hebrews 11:4). God
warns Cain: "sin lieth at the door" (v.7).

The First Murder (vv.8-16): Cain kills Abel in the field (v.8). God asks:
"Where is Abel thy brother?" (v.9). Cain's defiant answer: "Am I my brother's
keeper?" (v.9). Abel's blood "crieth unto me from the ground" (v.10). Cain is
cursed — a fugitive and vagabond (vv.11-12). God mercifully marks Cain for
protection (v.15). Cain goes "out from the presence of the LORD" (v.16).

Cain's Line (vv.17-24): Civilization develops: cities (v.17), polygamy (v.19),
nomadic herding (v.20), music (v.21), metalwork (v.22). But also escalating
violence: Lamech boasts of killing and claims seventy-sevenfold vengeance
(vv.23-24). Culture without God produces both achievement and brutality.

Seth's Line (vv.25-26): Seth born — "another seed instead of Abel" (v.25).
His son Enos born. "Then began men to call upon the name of the LORD" (v.26)
— the beginning of public worship.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• The "land of Nod" (v.16) — meaning "wandering" — is located east of Eden.
  The eastward movement continues the pattern of departure from God's presence.
• Cain builds a city (v.17), the first mention of urbanization in Scripture.
  The location is unspecified, but civilization develops away from Eden.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3-5            | Hebrews     | 11      | 4                  | "By faith Abel offered to God a more acceptable sacrifice than Cain." (ESV)
v.7              | James       | 1       | 15                 | "Sin when it is fully grown brings forth death." (ESV)
v.8              | 1 John      | 3       | 12                 | "Cain, who was of the evil one and murdered his brother." (ESV)
v.9              | Matthew     | 23      | 35                 | "From the blood of righteous Abel." (ESV)
v.10             | Hebrews     | 12      | 24                 | "The sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel." (ESV)
v.26             | Romans      | 10      | 13                 | "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-4/
  Guzik covers the two offerings, the first murder, Cain's line, and Seth's
  line as the line of faith.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Sin Lieth at the Door" on v.7 (MTP Vol. 5, No. 263)
  "Sin lieth at the door — like a crouching beast, waiting to spring. It is
  at YOUR door — not far away but right there. And its desire is for you —
  it wants to master you, consume you, destroy you. But God says: thou shalt
  rule over it. You are not helpless. Sin crouches, but you can conquer —
  by God's grace, by obedience, by vigilance. Don't open the door."
  Sermon: "The Blood of Abel" on v.10 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 623)
  "Abel's blood cried from the ground — for vengeance, for justice. But
  Hebrews 12:24 tells us Christ's blood 'speaketh better things than that
  of Abel.' Abel's blood cried 'Punish!' Christ's blood cries 'Forgive!'
  Abel's blood demanded justice; Christ's blood provides mercy. Both bloods
  speak — but Christ's speaks a better word."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "I have gotten a man from the LORD" / ESV: "I have gotten a man with the help of the LORD" — Hebrew "qaniti ish eth Yahweh." The Hebrew "eth" is ambiguous — it can mean "with" or be a direct object marker. ESV adds "with the help of" for clarity. Eve may have thought Cain was the promised seed (3:15). She was wrong — tragically.
v.2              | KJV         | "a tiller of the ground" / ESV: "a worker of the ground" — Hebrew "obed adamah." Both equivalent; ESV uses modern "worker" vs. KJV's "tiller."
v.3              | KJV         | "in process of time" / ESV: "In the course of time" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "miqets yamim" — at the end of days. A set time for worship/offering — possibly the first hint of regular worship.
v.4              | KJV         | "firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof" / ESV: "firstborn of his flock and of their fat portions" — Both equivalent. Abel brought the best (firstborn) and the richest part (fat). His offering cost him something and involved blood.
v.5              | KJV         | "Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell" / ESV: "Cain was very angry, and his face fell" — Hebrew "charah" (burned with anger) and "naphal panim" (face fell). ESV uses modern "angry" and "face" vs. KJV's archaic "wroth" and "countenance." Same meaning.
v.7              | KJV         | "sin lieth at the door" / ESV: "sin is crouching at the door" — Hebrew "chattath robets" — sin is crouching. ESV's "crouching" vividly captures the predatory image of "robets" (used of animals lying in wait). KJV's "lieth" is less vivid. masculine pronouns ("his"/"him") for sin; ESV uses neuter ("its"/ "it"). Hebrew "chattath" (sin) is feminine, but the participle "robets" is masculine — suggesting a personified predator.
v.8              | KJV         | "slew him" / ESV: "killed him" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "wayahargehu" — and he killed him. ESV uses common "killed"; KJV uses older "slew."
v.9              | KJV         | "Am I my brother's keeper?" / ESV: "Am I my brother's keeper?" — Both identical. Hebrew "hashomer achi anoki?" — The answer God implies: YES. You ARE responsible for your brother.
v.12             | KJV         | "a fugitive and a vagabond" / ESV: "a fugitive and a wanderer" — Hebrew "na wanad" — wandering and fleeing. ESV's "wanderer" is more neutral than KJV's "vagabond" (which carries negative connotations in modern English).
v.15             | KJV         | "set a mark upon Cain" / ESV: "put a mark on Cain" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "oth" — a sign. Not a curse mark but a protection mark. God's mercy even in judgment.
v.16             | KJV         | "the land of Nod" / ESV: "the land of Nod" — Both identical. Hebrew "nod" = wandering. Cain went to the land of wandering — fulfilling his sentence as a fugitive.
v.21             | KJV         | "the harp and organ" / ESV: "the lyre and pipe" — Hebrew "kinnor" (lyre/harp) and "ugab" (pipe/flute). ESV's "lyre and pipe" are more precise; KJV's "harp and organ" reflect 1611 English instrument names.
v.22             | KJV         | "an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron" / ESV: "he was the forger of all instruments of bronze and iron" — Hebrew "lotesh" — one who sharpens/forges. ESV's "forger" is more direct. Also, "brass" (KJV) vs. "bronze" (ESV) — Hebrew "nechosheth" means bronze or copper, not brass (an alloy unknown in the ancient world).
v.25             | KJV         | "another seed instead of Abel" / ESV: "another offspring instead of Abel" — Hebrew "zera" — seed, offspring, descendant. ESV's "offspring" is clearer for modern readers; KJV's "seed" is more literal.
v.26             | KJV         | "then began men to call upon the name of the LORD" / ESV: "At that time people began to call upon the name of the LORD" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "qara beshem Yahweh" — to proclaim, invoke, worship the name of Yahweh. Public, corporate worship begins. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | NET         | "I have created a man just as the Lord did!" — a strikingly different interpretation of Hebrew "qaniti ish eth Yahweh." Where KJV/ESV read "gotten a man with/from the LORD," NET reads Eve as claiming creative power parallel to God's — a theologically provocative rendering.
v.7              | NET         | "It desires to dominate you, but you must subdue it" — makes the power-struggle explicit. ASV: "sin coucheth at the door" — the archaic "coucheth" vividly renders the animal-crouching image of Hebrew "robets."
v.8              | NET         | "Let's go out to the field" — uniquely supplies Cain's words to Abel (following the Samaritan Pentateuch, LXX, and Syriac), filling a gap where the Hebrew Masoretic text has "Cain said to Abel" with no speech.
v.15             | ASV         | "appointed a sign for Cain" — "appointed" (rather than "set/put a mark") suggests a divinely ordained protective signal, keeping closer to the Hebrew "sim" (set, appoint).
v.26             | NET         | "people began to worship the Lord" — interprets "call upon the name" as "worship," making the cultic meaning explicit. KJV/ESV/ASV retain the more literal "call upon the name of the LORD/Jehovah."

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Cain and Abel     | The first brothers. Abel = "breath/vapor" (fragile life). Cain = "acquired/gotten." Their offerings reveal their hearts. Sin at the door — Sin personified as a crouching predator. It desires to master you but can be ruled through obedience. Am I my brother's keeper? — Cain's evasion of responsibility. The implied answer: yes, you are responsible for others. Blood cries from the ground — Innocent blood has a voice before God. He hears and responds to injustice. | 
Cain's line       | Civilization without God. Cultural achievement (cities, arts, technology) alongside moral decay (violence, polygamy). | 
Seth's line       | The line of faith. "Another seed instead of Abel." The line through which the Redeemer will come. Call upon the name of the LORD — Public worship of Yahweh. The beginning of corporate faith expression. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering" (v.4). God looks at
   the heart behind the offering, not just the offering itself. What is the
   condition of your heart when you worship, give, or serve?

2. "Sin lieth at the door" (v.7). Sin is crouching at YOUR door right now —
   waiting for an opportunity. Are you vigilant? Are you ruling over it, or
   is it ruling over you?

3. "Am I my brother's keeper?" (v.9). Yes — you are. You are responsible for
   others. Who is God calling you to care for, protect, or look after?

4. "Cain went out from the presence of the LORD" (v.16). The worst
   consequence of sin: separation from God's presence. Are you moving toward
   God or away from Him?

5. Two lines emerge: Cain's (culture without God) and Seth's (worship of
   God). Which line are you building? Achievement without God, or life
   centered on calling upon His name?

David Guzik — Genesis 4 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-4/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

5.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The genealogy from Adam to Noah traces ten generations, showing both long life and the relentless refrain 'and he died' — death reigns after the Fall.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 is the genealogy from Adam to Noah — ten
generations spanning approximately 1,656 years (using the ages given). This
is the "book of the generations of Adam" (v.1) — the second of eleven
"toledoth" (generations) sections that structure Genesis. The chapter traces
Seth's godly line (in contrast to Cain's line in ch.4) and demonstrates both
the reality of death ("and he died" — repeated 8 times) and the hope of life
(Enoch, who didn't die).

The Refrain of Death (vv.1-31): Each entry follows a pattern: lived X years,
begat a son, lived Y more years, begat sons and daughters, "and he died."
Eight times the phrase "and he died" tolls like a funeral bell — the
consequence of Genesis 3:19 playing out generation after generation.

The Exception: Enoch (vv.21-24): "Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for
God took him" (v.24). In a chapter dominated by death, one man doesn't die.
He "walked with God" — intimate, sustained fellowship — and God took him
directly to Himself. A preview of resurrection hope.

Noah's Birth (vv.28-32): Lamech names his son Noah ("rest/comfort"), saying:
"This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because
of the ground which the LORD hath cursed" (v.29). A prophetic hope that the
curse might be addressed.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• No specific geography in this chapter — it is a genealogy from Adam to Noah.
• The long lifespans and genealogical structure suggest humanity spreading
  across the earth, though no locations are named.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Luke        | 3       | 38                 | "Adam, the son of God." (ESV)
v.22             | Hebrews     | 11      | 5                  | "By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death." (ESV)
v.24             | Jude        | 1       | 14-15              | "Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied." (ESV)
v.29             | Genesis     | 8       | 21                 | "I will never again curse the ground because of man." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-5/
  Guzik covers the genealogy's purpose, the refrain of death, Enoch's
  translation, and Noah's prophetic name.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Enoch Walked with God" on v.24 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 635)
  "Enoch WALKED with God — not ran, not flew, not leaped — walked. The
  steady, daily, consistent pace of a life lived in God's company. Not
  occasional visits but habitual companionship. And the result: he was not,
  for God took him. Walking with God leads to being with God. The daily walk
  becomes the eternal presence. Start walking today — and one day, without
  interruption, you'll find yourself home."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him" / ESV: "This is the book of the generations of Adam. When God created man, he made him in the likeness of God" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "sepher toledoth" — the written record of the descendants. The second "toledoth" section in Genesis (the first is 2:4). ESV restructures for modern English word order.
v.2              | KJV         | "called their name Adam" / ESV: "named them Man" — Hebrew "Adam" means "man/mankind." ESV translates the name to show its meaning; KJV retains the proper name. Both are valid — "Adam" is both a name and the Hebrew word for "man."
v.3              | KJV         | "begat a son in his own likeness, after his image" / ESV: "fathered a son in his own likeness, after his image" — Both equivalent. KJV's "begat" vs. ESV's "fathered" — same Hebrew "yalad" (to beget/father). Adam's son bears Adam's (fallen) image, not just God's original image. The image of God continues but is now transmitted through a fallen nature.
v.22             | KJV         | "walked with God" / ESV: "walked with God" — Both identical. Hebrew "hithhallek eth ha-Elohim" — walked habitually with God. The hithpael (reflexive) form suggests ongoing, deliberate, intimate companionship.
v.24             | KJV         | "he was not; for God took him" / ESV: "he was not, for God took him" — Both identical. Hebrew "einennu ki laqach oto Elohim" — he was no more because God took/received him. Enoch was translated directly to God's presence without dying. v.27 — Methuselah's name may mean "when he dies, it shall come" — possibly prophetic of the flood. He died the year the flood came. Both KJV and ESV render the name identically.
v.29             | KJV         | "This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed" / ESV: "Out of the ground that the LORD has cursed, this one shall bring us relief from our work and from the painful toil of our hands" — Hebrew "yenachamenu" — shall comfort/bring relief. ESV restructures for clarity and uses "relief" and "painful toil" to make the connection to the curse of 3:17-19 more explicit. "Noah" — Hebrew "Noach" — rest, comfort. Related to "nacham" (comfort). TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | NET         | "the record of the family line of Adam" — renders Hebrew "sepher toledoth" as "record of the family line" rather than "book of the generations" (KJV/ESV/ASV), modernizing the genealogical formula.
v.2              | NET         | "named them 'humankind'" — translates Hebrew "Adam" as the common noun "humankind" rather than the proper name, emphasizing the collective sense (male and female together named "Adam/humanity").
v.24             | NET         | "he disappeared because God took him away" — uniquely renders "he was not" as "he disappeared," making the supernatural event more vivid and explicit for modern readers. Hebrew), clarifying that people looked for Enoch but could not locate him. - v.29 — ASV brackets "[which cometh]" to show this is supplied for clarity and not in the Hebrew, demonstrating ASV's practice of marking translator additions. This transparency about the Hebrew text is unique to ASV.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Toledoth          | "Generations/account." A structural marker in Genesis introducing a new section of family history. "And he died"  — The repeated refrain demonstrating death's universal reign since the fall. The wages of sin paid generation after generation. Walked with God — Intimate, sustained, habitual fellowship with God. A life lived in conscious companionship with the Creator. Translated/Taken — Enoch's removal from earth without dying. God took him directly to Himself — a preview of resurrection hope. In his own likeness — Adam's fallen image passed to his descendants. The image of God remains but is now transmitted through a sinful nature. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "And he died" (repeated 8 times). Death is the universal human experience
   since the fall. Does the reality of death create urgency in your life?
   Are you living with eternity in view?

2. "Enoch walked with God" (v.24). One man in a chapter of death didn't die
   — because he walked with God. What does "walking with God" look like for
   you daily? Is it habitual or occasional?

3. "In his own likeness, after his image" (v.3). We inherit a fallen nature.
   But through Christ, we are being renewed in God's image (Col 3:10). Are
   you being transformed, or just passing on the old nature?

4. "This same shall comfort us" (v.29). Even in a world under curse, there
   is hope for comfort and rest. Where do you find rest from the curse's
   effects? Ultimately, only in Christ.

5. The genealogy connects Adam to Noah — showing God's faithfulness across
   generations. God keeps His promises over centuries. Can you trust Him
   with your lifetime?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 5 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-5/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

6.ESV:
1. When man began to multiply on the face of the land and daughters were born to them,
2. the sons of God saw that the daughters of man were attractive. And they took as their wives any they chose.
3. Then the LORD said, “My Spirit shall not abide in man forever, for he is flesh: his days shall be 120 years.”
4. The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of man and they bore children to them. These were the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown.
5. The LORD saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually.
6. And the LORD regretted that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him to his heart.
7. So the LORD said, “I will blot out man whom I have created from the face of the land, man and animals and creeping things and birds of the heavens, for I am sorry that I have made them.”
8. But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.
9. These are the generations of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, blameless in his generation. Noah walked with God.
10. And Noah had three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
11. Now the earth was corrupt in God’s sight, and the earth was filled with violence.
12. And God saw the earth, and behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted their way on the earth.
13. And God said to Noah, “I have determined to make an end of all flesh, for the earth is filled with violence through them. Behold, I will destroy them with the earth.
14. Make yourself an ark of gopher wood. Make rooms in the ark, and cover it inside and out with pitch.
15. This is how you are to make it: the length of the ark 300 cubits, its breadth 50 cubits, and its height 30 cubits.
16. Make a roof for the ark, and finish it to a cubit above, and set the door of the ark in its side. Make it with lower, second, and third decks.
17. For behold, I will bring a flood of waters upon the earth to destroy all flesh in which is the breath of life under heaven. Everything that is on the earth shall die.
18. But I will establish my covenant with you, and you shall come into the ark, you, your sons, your wife, and your sons’ wives with you.
19. And of every living thing of all flesh, you shall bring two of every sort into the ark to keep them alive with you. They shall be male and female.
20. Of the birds according to their kinds, and of the animals according to their kinds, of every creeping thing of the ground, according to its kind, two of every sort shall come in to you to keep them alive.
21. Also take with you every sort of food that is eaten, and store it up. It shall serve as food for you and for them.”
22. Noah did this; he did all that God commanded him.
6.KJV:
1. And it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them,
2. That the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose.
3. And the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years.
4. There were giants in the earth in those days; and also after that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bare children to them, the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown.
5. And GOD saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually.
6. And it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart.
7. And the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I have made them.
8. But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD.
9. These are the generations of Noah: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God.
10. And Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
11. The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence.
12. And God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth.
13. And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth.
14. Make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch.
15. And this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth of it fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits.
16. A window shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above; and the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof; with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it.
17. And, behold, I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die.
18. But with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons’ wives with thee.
19. And of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee; they shall be male and female.
20. Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive.
21. And take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them.
22. Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he.

6.NET:
1. When humankind began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born to them,
2. the sons of God saw that the daughters of humankind were beautiful. Thus they took wives for themselves from any they chose.

8. But Noah found favor in the sight of the Lord.
9. This is the account of Noah. Noah was a godly man; he was blameless among his contemporaries. He walked with God.
10. Noah had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
11. The earth was ruined in the sight of God; the earth was filled with violence.
12. God saw the earth, and indeed it was ruined, for all living creatures on the earth were sinful.
13. So God said to Noah, “I have decided that all living creatures must die, for the earth is filled with violence because of them. Now I am about to destroy them and the earth.
14. Make for yourself an ark of cypress wood. Make rooms in the ark, and cover it with pitch inside and out.
15. This is how you should make it: The ark is to be 450 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high.
16. Make a roof for the ark and finish it, leaving 18 inches from the top. Put a door in the side of the ark, and make lower, middle, and upper decks.
17. I am about to bring floodwaters on the earth to destroy from under the sky all the living creatures that have the breath of life in them. Everything that is on the earth will die,
18. but I will confirm my covenant with you. You will enter the ark—you, your sons, your wife, and your sons’ wives with you.
19. You must bring into the ark two of every kind of living creature from all flesh, male and female, to keep them alive with you.

14. Make a ship of gopher wood. You shall make rooms in the ship, and shall seal it inside and outside with pitch.
15. This is how you shall make it. The length of the ship shall be three hundred cubits, its width fifty cubits, and its height thirty cubits.
16. You shall make a roof in the ship, and you shall finish it to a cubit upward. You shall set the door of the ship in its side. You shall make it with lower, second, and third levels.
