6.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Humanity's wickedness grieves God; He determines to send a flood but saves Noah by grace.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 6 describes the world's moral collapse before the
flood and God's decision to judge humanity while preserving Noah. The chapter
raises difficult interpretive questions (the "sons of God," the Nephilim) but
its main point is clear: sin had so corrupted humanity that God grieved having
made them. Yet grace prevails — "Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD"
(v.8). God provides a way of salvation even as He announces judgment.

The Corruption of Humanity (vv.1-7): "Sons of God" took "daughters of men"
(v.2) — debated: (1) fallen angels cohabiting with humans (cf. Jude 6-7,
2 Pet 2:4), (2) the godly line of Seth intermarrying with Cain's ungodly
line, (3) powerful rulers/tyrants taking women by force. The result: Nephilim
("giants/fallen ones," v.4) and total moral corruption. "Every imagination of
the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually" (v.5). God was "grieved
at his heart" (v.6) and determined to destroy (v.7).

Noah Finds Grace (vv.8-10): "But Noah found grace" (v.8) — the first mention
of grace in Scripture. Noah was "just" (righteous), "perfect in his
generations" (blameless among his contemporaries), and "walked with God" (v.9).

The Ark Commanded (vv.11-22): The earth was "corrupt" and "filled with
violence" (vv.11-12). God tells Noah His plan and commands him to build an ark
(vv.13-16): 300 cubits long (~450 feet), 50 wide (~75 feet), 30 high (~45
feet). God establishes His covenant with Noah (v.18). Two of every kind to be
preserved (vv.19-20). "Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him,
so did he" (v.22).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• No specific geography in this chapter — the focus is on universal human
  wickedness and God's command to build the ark.
• The flood narrative is global in scope; no particular region is named.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | 1 Peter     | 3       | 20                 | "God's patience waited in the days of Noah." (ESV)
v.5              | Matthew     | 15      | 19                 | "Out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery." (ESV)
v.8              | Hebrews     | 11      | 7                  | "By faith Noah, being warned by God, constructed an ark." (ESV)
v.9              | 2 Peter     | 2       | 5                  | "Noah, a herald of righteousness." (ESV)
v.18             | 1 Peter     | 3       | 20-21              | "Eight persons were brought safely through water." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-6/
  Guzik covers the "sons of God" debate, the Nephilim, total depravity,
  God's grief, Noah's grace, and the ark's construction.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Noah Found Grace" on v.8 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1310)
  "BUT Noah — the most important 'but' in the chapter. The whole world is
  corrupt, violent, evil continually — BUT Noah. In a sea of wickedness, one
  man stands. And how? He FOUND grace — he didn't earn it, didn't deserve it,
  didn't manufacture it. He found it. Grace found him. In the darkest hour of
  human history, grace was still available. It still is."
  Sermon: "Complete Obedience" on v.22 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 827)
  "According to ALL that God commanded him, so did he. Not some — all. Not
  mostly — completely. Not approximately — exactly. Noah didn't edit God's
  instructions. He didn't improve on the design. He didn't skip the hard
  parts. All that God commanded — so did he. This is the obedience that saves:
  complete, unquestioning, faithful."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.2              | KJV         | "sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair" / — Both equivalent. Hebrew "bene ha-Elohim." In Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7 this phrase refers to angels. The debate: are these fallen angels, godly Sethites, or human rulers? Each view has strengths and problems. KJV's "fair" and ESV's "attractive" both render Hebrew "toboth" (good/beautiful).
v.3              | KJV         | "My spirit shall not always strive with man" / ESV: "My Spirit shall not abide in man forever" — Hebrew "yadon" — debated: strive, contend, abide, remain. KJV reads it as God's patience contending with sinful man; ESV reads it as God's Spirit not remaining in/with man indefinitely. Both are legitimate readings of a difficult Hebrew word. "An hundred and twenty years" — either the remaining time before the flood or a new limit on human lifespan.
v.4              | KJV         | "giants" / ESV: "Nephilim" — Hebrew "Nephilim" — from "naphal" (to fall). ESV retains the Hebrew word untranslated; KJV translates it as "giants" (following the LXX "gigantes"). ESV's approach preserves the ambiguity of the original; the Nephilim may have been large but "fallen ones" is the root meaning.
v.5              | KJV         | "every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually" / ESV: "every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually" — Hebrew "yetser" — imagination, inclination, intention, formation. ESV's "intention" and KJV's "imagination" both capture aspects of this word. Five intensifiers: every, intention/ imagination, thoughts, only, continually. Total, comprehensive, unrelenting evil.
v.6              | KJV         | "it repented the LORD" / ESV: "the LORD regretted" — Hebrew "nacham" — to be grieved, to be sorry, to relent. ESV's "regretted" is more natural modern English; KJV's "repented" can confuse readers (God doesn't repent of sin). Not that God changed His mind (Num 23:19) but that He experienced genuine grief. Anthropopathic language — describing God's emotions in human terms.
v.7              | KJV         | "I will destroy man" / ESV: "I will blot out man" — Hebrew "emcheh" — to wipe away, blot out, erase. ESV's "blot out" is more literal to the Hebrew; KJV's "destroy" captures the effect.
v.8              | KJV         | "grace" / ESV: "favor" — Hebrew "chen" — favor, grace, kindness. Both are valid translations. KJV's "grace" carries stronger theological weight in English; ESV's "favor" is more literal. First occurrence of "chen" in Scripture. Noah didn't earn favor — he found it. Grace precedes righteousness.
v.9              | KJV         | "a just man and perfect in his generations" / ESV: "a righteous man, blameless in his generation" — Hebrew "tsaddiq" (righteous/just) and "tamim" (blameless/complete/without blemish). ESV's "blameless" is clearer than KJV's "perfect" (which modern readers may take as sinless). "Tamim" means whole, complete, having integrity — not sinless perfection.
v.14             | KJV         | "gopher wood" / ESV: "gopher wood" — Both identical. Unknown wood type. Possibly cypress. "Pitch" — Hebrew "kopher" — same root as "atonement" (kaphar). The ark was "atoned" — covered/sealed. v.15 — The ark's dimensions (300 x 50 x 30 cubits) give a ratio of 6:1:0.6 — remarkably stable for a vessel. Both translations render the measurements identically.
v.16             | KJV         | "A window shalt thou make" / ESV: "Make a roof for the ark" — Hebrew "tsohar" — debated: window, roof opening, or light source. KJV reads it as "window"; ESV reads it as "roof." The Hebrew is uncertain.
v.22             | KJV         | "Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he" / ESV: "Noah did this; he did all that God commanded him" — Both equivalent. Complete, exact obedience. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.3              | NET         | "My Spirit will not remain in humankind indefinitely, since they are mortal" — interprets difficult Hebrew "yadon" as "remain" and adds "since they are mortal" as an explanatory gloss. Also: "They will remain for 120 more years" — makes the countdown to judgment explicit. - v.4 — ASV retains "Nephilim" untranslated (like ESV), while NET renders the sexual union bluntly: "the sons of God would sleep with the daughters of humankind" — making the transgression graphic where KJV/ESV use "came in to."
v.6              | NET         | "he was highly offended" — a strikingly different rendering of Hebrew "itstsabon" (KJV: "it grieved him at his heart" / ESV: "it grieved him to his heart"). NET shifts from grief/sorrow to offense/anger.
v.14             | NET         | "cypress wood" — translates "gopher wood" as "cypress" where all others leave it untranslated, reflecting a scholarly identification. function as a seagoing vessel. - v.15 — NET converts cubits to modern measurements (450 feet, 75 feet, 45 feet), making dimensions immediately accessible. Other translations retain the ancient cubit measurements.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Sons of God       | Debated figures who intermarried with "daughters of men." Options: fallen angels, godly Sethites, or powerful rulers. | 
Nephilim          | "Giants" or "fallen ones." Mighty beings of the pre-flood world. Their exact nature remains debated. Every imagination only evil continually — Total depravity. The complete corruption of human thought and desire before the flood. It repented the LORD — God's genuine grief over sin. Not a change of mind but deep emotional response to humanity's corruption. Noah found grace — The first mention of grace in Scripture. Unmerited favor given to one man in a world of wickedness. | 
The ark           | God's provision of salvation through judgment. A vessel of preservation — one door, one way of escape. According to all that God commanded — Complete, exact obedience. Noah did everything God said, exactly as God said it. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil
   continually" (v.5). This describes humanity without God's restraint. What
   does your thought life look like? Are you guarding your mind or letting
   it run unchecked?

2. "Noah found grace" (v.8). In the worst generation in history, grace was
   available. No matter how dark your world, grace is findable. Have you
   found it?

3. "Noah walked with God" (v.9). Like Enoch before him (5:24), Noah walked
   with God in a corrupt generation. You don't need a godly culture to walk
   with God — you can walk with Him anywhere. Are you?

4. "According to all that God commanded him, so did he" (v.22). Noah's
   obedience was complete — not partial, not selective. Is your obedience
   complete? Or do you obey the comfortable commands and ignore the hard
   ones?

5. The ark had one door (v.16). There was one way of salvation. Jesus said:
   "I am the door" (John 10:9). Have you entered through the one door?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 6 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-6/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

7.ESV:
1. Then the LORD said to Noah, “Go into the ark, you and all your household, for I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation.
2. Take with you seven pairs of all clean animals, the male and his mate, and a pair of the animals that are not clean, the male and his mate,
3. and seven pairs of the birds of the heavens also, male and female, to keep their offspring alive on the face of all the earth.
4. For in seven days I will send rain on the earth forty days and forty nights, and every living thing that I have made I will blot out from the face of the ground.”
5. And Noah did all that the LORD had commanded him.
6. Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters came upon the earth.
7. And Noah and his sons and his wife and his sons’ wives with him went into the ark to escape the waters of the flood.
8. Of clean animals, and of animals that are not clean, and of birds, and of everything that creeps on the ground,
9. two and two, male and female, went into the ark with Noah, as God had commanded Noah.
10. And after seven days the waters of the flood came upon the earth.
11. In the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains of the great deep burst forth, and the windows of the heavens were opened.
12. And rain fell upon the earth forty days and forty nights.
13. On the very same day Noah and his sons, Shem and Ham and Japheth, and Noah’s wife and the three wives of his sons with them entered the ark,
14. they and every beast, according to its kind, and all the livestock according to their kinds, and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth, according to its kind, and every bird, according to its kind, every winged creature.
15. They went into the ark with Noah, two and two of all flesh in which there was the breath of life.
16. And those that entered, male and female of all flesh, went in as God had commanded him. And the LORD shut him in.
17. The flood continued forty days on the earth. The waters increased and bore up the ark, and it rose high above the earth.
18. The waters prevailed and increased greatly on the earth, and the ark floated on the face of the waters.
19. And the waters prevailed so mightily on the earth that all the high mountains under the whole heaven were covered.
20. The waters prevailed above the mountains, covering them fifteen cubits deep.
21. And all flesh died that moved on the earth, birds, livestock, beasts, all swarming creatures that swarm on the earth, and all mankind.
22. Everything on the dry land in whose nostrils was the breath of life died.
23. He blotted out every living thing that was on the face of the ground, man and animals and creeping things and birds of the heavens. They were blotted out from the earth. Only Noah was left, and those who were with him in the ark.
24. And the waters prevailed on the earth 150 days.
7.KJV:
1. And the LORD said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation.
2. Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female.
3. Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth.
4. For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth.
5. And Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him.
6. And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth.

9. There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah.

11. In the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened.
12. And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights.
13. In the selfsame day entered Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah’s wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark;
14. They, and every beast after his kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind, and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort.
15. And they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life.
16. And they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him: and the LORD shut him in.

21. And all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man:

23. And every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl of the heaven; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark.
24. And the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days.
7.ASV:
1. And Jehovah said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation.
2. Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee seven and seven, the male and his female; and of the beasts that are not clean two, the male and his female:

8. Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of birds, and of everything that creepeth upon the ground,
9. there went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, male and female, as God commanded Noah.
10. And it came to pass after the seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth.
11. In the six hundredth year of Noah`s life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened.
12. And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights.
13. In the selfsame day entered Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah`s wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark;
14. they, and every beast after its kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth after its kind, and every bird after its kind, every bird of every sort.
15. And they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh wherein is the breath of life.
16. And they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God commanded him: and Jehovah shut him in.
17. And the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lifted up above the earth.
18. And the waters prevailed, and increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters.
19. And the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high mountains that were under the whole heaven were covered.
20. Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered.
21. And all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both birds, and cattle, and beasts, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man:
22. all in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, of all that was on the dry land, died.
23. And every living thing was destroyed that was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and creeping things, and birds of the heavens; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only was left, and they that were with him in the ark.
24. And the waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days.

7.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Noah, his family, and the animals enter the ark; God sends the flood and destroys all life on earth.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 7 records the flood itself — God's judgment on a
corrupt world and His preservation of Noah's family and the animals. The flood
is presented as a de-creation: the waters above and below (separated in 1:6-7)
come back together, returning the earth to its pre-creation watery state. It
is both judgment (destruction of the wicked) and salvation (preservation of
the righteous). The NT uses the flood as a type of both baptism (1 Pet 3:20-21)
and future judgment (2 Pet 3:5-7).

Entering the Ark (vv.1-10): God invites Noah: "Come thou and all thy house
into the ark" (v.1) — "come" not "go" (God is already there). Clean animals
by sevens; unclean by twos (v.2). Seven days' warning before the rain (v.4).
"Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him" (v.5). Noah was 600
years old when the flood came (v.6).

The Flood Begins (vv.11-16): Precise dating: "the six hundredth year of
Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day" (v.11). Two sources:
"the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were
opened" (v.11). Rain for forty days and nights (v.12). "The LORD shut him in"
(v.16) — God Himself closed the door.

The Flood's Devastation (vv.17-24): Waters increased, lifted the ark (v.17).
Waters prevailed "exceedingly" — covering all high hills and mountains (vv.19-
20). "All flesh died" — every land creature and every person outside the ark
(vv.21-23). "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark"
(v.23). Waters prevailed 150 days (v.24).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• No specific geography — the flood covers the entire earth (vv.19-20).
• The "fountains of the great deep" and "windows of heaven" (v.11) describe
  waters from below and above engulfing all known geography.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Hebrews     | 11      | 7                  | "By faith Noah, being warned by God, constructed an ark for the saving of his household." (ESV)
v.4              | Matthew     | 24      | 37-39              | "As were the days of Noah, so will be the coming of the Son of Man." (ESV)
v.16             | Psalm       | 91      | 1                  | "He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty." (ESV)
v.23             | 2 Peter     | 3       | 6                  | "The world that then existed was deluged with water and perished." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-7/
  Guzik covers the invitation to the ark, the flood's sources, the
  devastation, and the typological significance.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The LORD Shut Him In" on v.16 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 634)
  "The LORD shut him in — not Noah. Noah didn't secure his own safety — God
  did. The same hand that shut Noah IN shut the world OUT. When God closes
  the door, no one can open it. When God secures you, no one can unsecure
  you. Your salvation depends not on your grip on the door handle but on
  God's hand that shut the door. He shut him in — safe, secure, sealed by
  God Himself."
  Sermon: "Come Into the Ark" on v.1 (MTP Vol. 20, No. 1201)
  "COME — not 'go.' God says 'come' because He is already in the ark. He
  doesn't send Noah away from Himself but invites him toward Himself. Christ
  says the same: 'Come unto me.' Not 'go somewhere' but 'come to where I am.'
  The ark is where God is — and He invites you in."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "Come thou and all thy house into the ark" / ESV: "Go into the ark, you and all your household" — Hebrew "bo" — come/enter. This is a significant translation difference. KJV renders "bo" as "come" (implying God is already in the ark, inviting Noah toward Himself); ESV renders it as "go" (a directional command). The Hebrew "bo" most naturally means "come/enter" — KJV's rendering preserves the theological nuance that God invites Noah toward His presence.
v.2              | KJV         | "by sevens, the male and his female" / ESV: "seven pairs... the male and his mate" — Hebrew "shib'ah shib'ah" — seven seven. Debated: seven total or seven pairs? ESV interprets as "seven pairs" (14 total); KJV's "by sevens" leaves it ambiguous. The distinction between clean and unclean animals predates the Mosaic Law.
v.3              | KJV         | "to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth" / ESV: "to keep their offspring alive on the face of all the earth" — Hebrew "zera" — seed, offspring. ESV's "offspring" is clearer for modern readers.
v.4              | KJV         | "every living substance that I have made will I destroy" / ESV: "every living thing that I have made I will blot out" — Hebrew "emcheh" — to wipe away, blot out. ESV's "blot out" is more literal; KJV's "destroy" captures the result. "Forty days and forty nights" — both identical. Forty is a number of testing/judgment throughout Scripture.
v.11             | KJV         | "all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened" / ESV: "all the fountains of the great deep burst forth, and the windows of the heavens were opened" — Hebrew "nib'qe'u" — burst open, split apart. ESV's "burst forth" is more vivid than KJV's "broken up." Both convey catastrophic eruption of subterranean waters.
v.16             | KJV         | "the LORD shut him in" / ESV: "the LORD shut him in" — Both identical. Hebrew "Yahweh sagar ba'ado" — Yahweh closed [the door] behind him. God personally sealed the ark. Divine security.
v.19             | KJV         | "all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered" / ESV: "all the high mountains under the whole heaven were covered" — Hebrew "harim" — hills or mountains. ESV's "mountains" is stronger; KJV's "hills" may understate the scope. The language indicates a universal, global flood — "under the whole heaven" = comprehensive.
v.23             | KJV         | "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark" / ESV: "Only Noah was left, and those who were with him in the ark" — Both equivalent. Salvation exclusively through the ark.
v.24             | KJV         | "an hundred and fifty days" / ESV: "150 days" — Both identical in meaning. Five months of prevailing waters. The flood was not a brief rainstorm but a prolonged, catastrophic event. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------ - v.1 — ASV/WEB both retain "Come" (Hebrew "bo"), preserving the theological nuance that God invites Noah toward Himself. NET also uses "Come into the ark." WEB renders "ark" as "ship" throughout the flood narrative.
v.1              | NET         | "I consider you godly" — interprets "righteous" (KJV/ESV) as "godly," shifting from legal/moral standing to character description.
v.11             | NET         | "the floodgates of the heavens were opened" — renders Hebrew "arubboth hashamayim" as "floodgates" (vs. "windows" in KJV/ESV/ASV/WEB), a more hydrological image that avoids the literal "windows" metaphor.
v.19             | ASV         | "all the high mountains that were under the whole heaven were covered" — uses "mountains" (like ESV) where KJV has weaker "hills," strengthening the global scope of the flood. - v.20 — NET converts "fifteen cubits" to "more than 20 feet," continuing its practice of modernizing measurements for accessibility.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Forty days        | A period of testing/judgment. The duration of the rain (though the flood lasted much longer overall). Fountains of the deep — Subterranean water sources that erupted during the flood. The earth's internal waters released. The LORD shut him in — God personally sealing Noah in the ark. Divine security — God closes the door of salvation. Noah only remained — Salvation exclusively through the ark. No other means of escape. One way of salvation. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Come thou... into the ark" (v.1). God says "come" — toward Himself. Are
   you responding to His invitation? He's not sending you away; He's
   inviting you in.

2. "The LORD shut him in" (v.16). God secures your salvation — not you. Your
   security depends on His hand, not yours. Does this give you confidence?

3. "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark"
   (v.23). There was only one way of salvation — the ark. There is only one
   way now — Christ. Have you entered?

4. "As the days of Noe were, so shall also the coming of the Son of man be"
   (Matt 24:37). Jesus compared His return to Noah's flood — sudden judgment
   on an unprepared world. Are you prepared?

5. Noah obeyed completely (v.5) and was saved. Obedience is the response to
   grace. God provides the ark (grace); you must enter it (obedience). Have
   you done both — received grace AND responded with obedience?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 7 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-7/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

8.ESV:
1. But God remembered Noah and all the beasts and all the livestock that were with him in the ark. And God made a wind blow over the earth, and the waters subsided.
2. The fountains of the deep and the windows of the heavens were closed, the rain from the heavens was restrained,
3. and the waters receded from the earth continually. At the end of 150 days the waters had abated,
4. and in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat.
5. And the waters continued to abate until the tenth month; in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains were seen.
6. At the end of forty days Noah opened the window of the ark that he had made
7. and sent forth a raven. It went to and fro until the waters were dried up from the earth.
8. Then he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters had subsided from the face of the ground.
9. But the dove found no place to set her foot, and she returned to him to the ark, for the waters were still on the face of the whole earth. So he put out his hand and took her and brought her into the ark with him.
10. He waited another seven days, and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark.
11. And the dove came back to him in the evening, and behold, in her mouth was a freshly plucked olive leaf. So Noah knew that the waters had subsided from the earth.
12. Then he waited another seven days and sent forth the dove, and she did not return to him anymore.
13. In the six hundred and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried from off the earth. And Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold, the face of the ground was dry.
14. In the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the earth had dried out.
15. Then God said to Noah,
16. “Go out from the ark, you and your wife, and your sons and your sons’ wives with you.
17. Bring out with you every living thing that is with you of all flesh—birds and animals and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth—that they may swarm on the earth, and be fruitful and multiply on the earth.”
18. So Noah went out, and his sons and his wife and his sons’ wives with him.
19. Every beast, every creeping thing, and every bird, everything that moves on the earth, went out by families from the ark.
20. Then Noah built an altar to the LORD and took some of every clean animal and some of every clean bird and offered burnt offerings on the altar.
21. And when the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma, the LORD said in his heart, “I will never again curse the ground because of man, for the intention of man’s heart is evil from his youth. Neither will I ever again strike down every living creature as I have done.
22. While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night, shall not cease.”
8.KJV:
1. And God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged;
2. The fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained;
3. And the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated.
4. And the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat.
5. And the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen.
6. And it came to pass at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made:
7. And he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth.
8. Also he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground;
9. But the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark, for the waters were on the face of the whole earth: then he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark.
10. And he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark;
11. And the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth.
12. And he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more.
13. And it came to pass in the six hundredth and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dry.
14. And in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried.
15. And God spake unto Noah, saying,
16. Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons’ wives with thee.
17. Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth.
18. And Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons’ wives with him:
19. Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark.
20. And Noah builded an altar unto the LORD; and took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl, and offered burnt offerings on the altar.
21. And the LORD smelled a sweet savour; and the LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man’s sake; for the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done.
22. While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease.

8.NET:
1. But God remembered Noah and all the wild animals and domestic animals that were with him in the ark. God caused a wind to blow over the earth and the waters receded.
2. The fountains of the deep and the floodgates of heaven were closed, and the rain stopped falling from the sky.
3. The waters kept receding steadily from the earth, so that they had gone down by the end of the 150 days.
4. On the seventeenth day of the seventh month, the ark came to rest on one of the mountains of Ararat.
5. The waters kept on receding until the tenth month. On the first day of the tenth month, the tops of the mountains became visible.
6. At the end of 40 days, Noah opened the window he had made in the ark
7. and sent out a raven; it kept flying back and forth until the waters had dried up on the earth.

9. The dove could not find a resting place for its feet because water still covered the surface of the entire earth, and so it returned to Noah in the ark. He stretched out his hand, took the dove, and brought it back into the ark.
10. He waited seven more days and then sent out the dove again from the ark.
11. When the dove returned to him in the evening, there was a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak! Noah knew that the waters had receded from the earth.
12. He waited another seven days and sent the dove out again, but it did not return to him this time.
13. In Noah’s six hundred and first year, in the first day of the first month, the waters had dried up from the earth, and Noah removed the covering from the ark and saw that the surface of the ground was dry.
14. And by the twenty-seventh day of the second month the earth was dry.
15. Then God spoke to Noah and said,
16. “Come out of the ark, you, your wife, your sons, and your sons’ wives with you.
17. Bring out with you all the living creatures that are with you. Bring out every living thing, including the birds, animals, and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth. Let them increase and be fruitful and multiply on the earth!”
18. Noah went out along with his sons, his wife, and his sons’ wives.
19. Every living creature, every creeping thing, every bird, and everything that moves on the earth went out of the ark in their groups.
20. Noah built an altar to the Lord. He then took some of every kind of clean animal and clean bird and offered burnt offerings on the altar.
21. And the Lord smelled the soothing aroma and said to himself, “I will never again curse the ground because of humankind, even though the inclination of their minds is evil from childhood on. I will never again destroy everything that lives, as I have just done.
22. “While the earth continues to exist, planting time and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, and day and night will not cease.”

14. In the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the earth was dry.
15. God spoke to Noah, saying,
16. “Go out of the ship, you, your wife, your sons, and your sons’ wives with you.
17. Bring out with you every living thing that is with you of all flesh, including birds, livestock, and every creeping thing that creeps on the earth, that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply on the earth.”
18. Noah went out, with his sons, his wife, and his sons’ wives with him.
19. Every animal, every creeping thing, and every bird, whatever moves on the earth, after their families, went out of the ship.
20. Noah built an altar to Yahweh, and took of every clean animal, and of every clean bird, and offered burnt offerings on the altar.
21. Yahweh smelled the pleasant aroma. Yahweh said in his heart, “I will not again curse the ground any more for man’s sake because the imagination of man’s heart is evil from his youth. I will never again strike every living thing, as I have done.
22. While the earth remains, seed time and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night will not cease.”
8.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The floodwaters recede, Noah sends out birds to test the land, and finally exits the ark to offer sacrifice to God.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 8 records the flood's recession and Noah's exit
from the ark. After the de-creation of chapter 7, chapter 8 is a re-creation
— God restoring the earth through wind (Spirit), separating waters from land,
and commanding life to fill the earth again. The chapter ends with Noah's
sacrifice and God's promise never to flood the earth again. The flood lasted
approximately one year and ten days total.

God Remembers Noah (vv.1-5): "God remembered Noah" (v.1) — not that He forgot
but that He now acts on Noah's behalf. God sends a wind (Hebrew "ruach" —
same word as "Spirit" in 1:2) over the earth (v.1). Waters recede. The ark
rests on Ararat on the 7th month, 17th day (v.4). Mountains visible by the
10th month (v.5).

The Birds (vv.6-12): After 40 more days, Noah sends a raven — it flies back
and forth (v.7). He sends a dove — it returns (no dry land, v.9). Seven days
later, the dove returns with an olive leaf — waters receding (v.11). Seven
more days — the dove doesn't return — dry land (v.12).

Exiting the Ark (vv.13-19): On the 1st month, 1st day of Noah's 601st year,
the ground is dry (v.13). God commands: "Go forth" (v.16). All creatures exit
to "be fruitful, and multiply" (v.17) — echoing 1:22, 28.

Noah's Sacrifice and God's Promise (vv.20-22): Noah builds an altar — the
first altar in Scripture (v.20). He offers clean animals as burnt offerings
(v.20). God smells the "sweet savour" and promises: never again curse the
ground or destroy all living things (v.21). Seasons will continue as long as
the earth remains (v.22).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• The ark rests on the "mountains of Ararat" (v.4) — in the region of modern
  eastern Turkey/Armenia. Ararat is a region, not necessarily the specific
  peak known today as Mount Ararat.
• The waters recede to reveal dry land — a new beginning geographically
  paralleling the original creation emerging from the waters (cf. 1:9).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Exodus      | 2       | 24                 | "God remembered his covenant with Abraham." (ESV)
v.11             | Romans      | 5       | 1                  | "We have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." (ESV)
v.20             | Leviticus   | 1       | 9                  | "A burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD." (ESV)
v.21             | Ephesians   | 5       | 2                  | "Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God." (ESV)
v.22             | Jeremiah    | 33      | 20-21              | "If you can break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-8/
  Guzik covers God's remembrance, the re-creation parallels, the birds,
  Noah's sacrifice, and God's promise.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "God Remembered Noah" on v.1 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1311)
  "God REMEMBERED Noah — what comfort in these words! In the midst of
  judgment, God remembers His own. The waters raged, the world perished, but
  God remembered the man in the ark. He remembers you too — in your trial,
  in your waiting, in your darkness. He has not forgotten. He remembers — and
  when He remembers, He acts."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "God remembered Noah" / ESV: "God remembered Noah" — Both identical. Hebrew "zakar" — to remember, to act on behalf of. Not recollection (God doesn't forget) but action. When God "remembers," He intervenes.
v.1              | KJV         | "God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged" / ESV: "God made a wind blow over the earth, and the waters subsided" — Hebrew "ruach" — wind/spirit/breath. Same word as Genesis 1:2 ("Spirit of God"). The re-creation parallel is intentional. KJV's archaic "asswaged" = ESV's "subsided" — both mean the waters receded.
v.4              | KJV         | "the ark rested... upon the mountains of Ararat" / ESV: "the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "nuach" — same root as "Noah." Noah rests on the mountain. Ararat is a mountain range in modern eastern Turkey (not a single peak).
v.9              | KJV         | "the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot" / ESV: "the dove found no place to set her foot" — Hebrew "manoach" — resting place. KJV is more literal ("rest for the sole of her foot"); ESV paraphrases for clarity ("no place to set her foot"). Same meaning.
v.11             | KJV         | "an olive leaf pluckt off" / ESV: "a freshly plucked olive leaf" — Both equivalent. ESV adds "freshly" to clarify the leaf was newly plucked (indicating active growth). The olive tree can survive flooding and sprout quickly.
v.17             | KJV         | "that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply" / ESV: "that they may swarm on the earth, and be fruitful and multiply" — Hebrew "sharats" — to swarm, teem. ESV's "swarm" is more literal; KJV's "breed abundantly" interprets the result.
v.20             | KJV         | "burnt offerings" / ESV: "burnt offerings" — Both identical. Hebrew "oloth" — whole burnt offerings. Completely consumed by fire — total dedication to God. The first altar and first formal sacrifice recorded.
v.21             | KJV         | "a sweet savour" / ESV: "the pleasing aroma" — Hebrew "reach nichoach" — a soothing/restful aroma. ESV's "pleasing aroma" is more natural modern English; KJV's "sweet savour" is archaic but evocative. God is "satisfied" by the sacrifice — a picture of Christ's sacrifice satisfying God (Eph 5:2).
v.21             | KJV         | "I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake" / equivalent. KJV's "for man's sake" could be misread as "for man's benefit"; ESV's "because of man" is clearer.
v.21             | KJV         | "the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth" / ESV: "the intention of man's heart is evil from his youth" — Hebrew "yetser" — imagination, inclination, intention. Same word as 6:5. The same diagnosis as before, but now the basis for mercy rather than judgment. God knows our nature and chooses grace.
v.22             | KJV         | "While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease" / ESV: "While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night, shall not cease" — Both essentially identical. God's guarantee of creation's stability. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.4              | NET         | "the ark came to rest on one of the mountains of Ararat" — adds "one of" to clarify the Hebrew plural "mountains of Ararat" refers to a range, not a single peak. Other translations leave this ambiguous.
v.21             | NET         | "the inclination of their minds is evil from childhood on" — renders Hebrew "yetser" as "inclination of their minds" (vs. "imagination of man's heart" KJV) and "from his youth" as "from childhood on," making the doctrine of innate depravity more psychologically explicit.
v.21             | NET         | "said to himself" — renders Hebrew "said in his heart" as "said to himself," a more natural English idiom for internal resolve. ASV/WEB retain the more literal "said in his heart."
v.22             | NET         | "While the earth continues to exist" — a more dynamic rendering than "While the earth remaineth" (KJV/ASV), subtly acknowledging the earth has a future end point.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Wind/Ruach        | The same word as "Spirit." God uses wind to recede the waters — paralleling the Spirit hovering over waters in 1:2. | 
Ararat            | The mountain range where the ark rested. In modern Turkey. "Rested" (nuach) connects to Noah's name. | 
Olive leaf        | The sign of new life and receding waters. Later associated with peace and hope. Burnt offering — A sacrifice completely consumed by fire. Total dedication to God. Noah's first act after deliverance: worship. | 
Sweet savour      | God's satisfaction with the sacrifice. A picture of how Christ's sacrifice satisfies God's justice. Seedtime and harvest — God's guarantee of creation's stability. The seasons will continue until the end. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "God remembered Noah" (v.1). In your waiting — when it feels like God has
   forgotten — He hasn't. He remembers. And when He remembers, He acts. Can
   you trust His timing?

2. Noah's first act after deliverance was worship (v.20). What is YOUR first
   response to God's deliverance — gratitude and worship, or moving on to
   the next thing?

3. "The imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth" (v.21). God knows
   our sinful nature — and STILL chooses grace. His mercy is not based on
   our goodness but on His character. Does this humble you?

4. "While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest... shall not cease"
   (v.22). God guarantees creation's stability. Every sunrise, every season,
   every harvest is God keeping His promise. Do you notice His faithfulness
   in the ordinary rhythms of life?

5. The flood is both judgment and salvation — destruction of the wicked and
   preservation of the righteous. God's actions always have both dimensions.
   Are you on the salvation side?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 8 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-8/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

9.ESV:
1. And God blessed Noah and his sons and said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth.
2. The fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth and upon every bird of the heavens, upon everything that creeps on the ground and all the fish of the sea. Into your hand they are delivered.
3. Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you. And as I gave you the green plants, I give you everything.
4. But you shall not eat flesh with its life, that is, its blood.
5. And for your lifeblood I will require a reckoning: from every beast I will require it and from man. From his fellow man I will require a reckoning for the life of man.
6. “Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own image.
7. And you, be fruitful and multiply, increase greatly on the earth and multiply in it.”
8. Then God said to Noah and to his sons with him,
9. “Behold, I establish my covenant with you and your offspring after you,
10. and with every living creature that is with you, the birds, the livestock, and every beast of the earth with you, as many as came out of the ark; it is for every beast of the earth.
11. I establish my covenant with you, that never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of the flood, and never again shall there be a flood to destroy the earth.”
12. And God said, “This is the sign of the covenant that I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for all future generations:
13. I have set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and the earth.
14. When I bring clouds over the earth and the bow is seen in the clouds,
15. I will remember my covenant that is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh. And the waters shall never again become a flood to destroy all flesh.
16. When the bow is in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is on the earth.”
17. God said to Noah, “This is the sign of the covenant that I have established between me and all flesh that is on the earth.”
18. The sons of Noah who went forth from the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. (Ham was the father of Canaan.)
19. These three were the sons of Noah, and from these the people of the whole earth were dispersed.
20. Noah began to be a man of the soil, and he planted a vineyard.
21. He drank of the wine and became drunk and lay uncovered in his tent.
22. And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father and told his two brothers outside.
23. Then Shem and Japheth took a garment, laid it on both their shoulders, and walked backward and covered the nakedness of their father. Their faces were turned backward, and they did not see their father’s nakedness.
24. When Noah awoke from his wine and knew what his youngest son had done to him,
25. he said, “Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be to his brothers.”
26. He also said, “Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem; and let Canaan be his servant.
27. May God enlarge Japheth, and let him dwell in the tents of Shem, and let Canaan be his servant.”
28. After the flood Noah lived 350 years.
29. All the days of Noah were 950 years, and he died.

9.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God establishes a covenant with Noah, sets the rainbow as its sign, and gives new commands for post-flood humanity.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 9 records God's covenant with Noah — the first
explicit covenant in Scripture. God re-commissions humanity (echoing 1:28),
establishes new regulations for the post-flood world (permission to eat meat,
prohibition of blood, capital punishment for murder), and seals His promise
with the rainbow. The chapter also records Noah's failure (drunkenness) and
the resulting prophecy over his three sons — shaping the rest of biblical
history.

The Noahic Covenant (vv.1-17):
- Re-commission: "Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth" (v.1).
- New authority: animals now fear humans (v.2).
- New diet: permission to eat meat (v.3) — but not blood (v.4).
- Sanctity of life: murder requires capital punishment because man bears God's
  image (vv.5-6).
- The covenant promise: never again a universal flood (v.11).
- The sign: the rainbow (vv.12-17).

Noah's Failure (vv.20-23): Noah plants a vineyard, gets drunk, lies naked in
his tent (vv.20-21). Ham "saw the nakedness of his father" and told his
brothers (v.22) — likely involving more than just seeing (the Hebrew may imply
disrespect or exploitation). Shem and Japheth cover their father respectfully
(v.23).

The Prophecy (vv.24-27): Noah curses Canaan (Ham's son) — "a servant of
servants" (v.25). Blesses Shem — "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem" (v.26) —
the Messianic line. Blesses Japheth — "God shall enlarge Japheth, and he
shall dwell in the tents of Shem" (v.27) — Gentile inclusion.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• No specific geography — the focus is on God's covenant with Noah and the
  post-flood commands for all humanity.
• The prophecy over Shem, Ham, and Japheth (vv.25-27) sets up the Table of
  Nations (ch.10) and the geographical spread of peoples.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Genesis     | 1       | 28                 | "Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth." (ESV)
v.6              | James       | 3       | 9                  | "People who are made in the likeness of God." (ESV)
v.9-17           | Isaiah      | 54      | 9-10               | "This is like the days of Noah to me: as I swore that the waters of Noah should no more go over the earth." (ESV)
v.11             | Revelation  | 21      | 1                  | "The first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-9/
  Guzik covers the Noahic covenant, the rainbow, Noah's failure, and the
  prophecy over the three sons.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Rainbow" on v.13 (MTP Vol. 9, No. 527)
  "I do set MY bow in the cloud — God's bow, not man's. A weapon of war
  (the bow) now pointed away from earth, toward heaven — as if God has laid
  down His weapon. The rainbow is God's promise made visible: I will not
  destroy again. Every rainbow is a sermon — preaching God's faithfulness,
  God's mercy, God's covenant-keeping character."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "replenish the earth" / ESV: "fill the earth" — Hebrew "mil'u" — to fill. Same word and same issue as 1:28. ESV's "fill" is more accurate; KJV's "replenish" in 1611 meant "fill" but modern readers may misread it as "refill."
v.3              | KJV         | "every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you" / ESV: "Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you" — Hebrew "oklah" — food. KJV's "meat" meant "food" in 1611 (not specifically animal flesh). ESV's "food" avoids confusion. Before the flood, humanity was apparently vegetarian (1:29). Now meat is permitted — but not blood.
v.4              | KJV         | "flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat" / ESV: "But you shall not eat flesh with its life, that is, its blood" — Both equivalent. Blood represents life. Consuming blood is prohibited because life belongs to God. This prohibition predates and transcends the Mosaic Law.
v.5              | KJV         | "at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man" / ESV: "From his fellow man I will require a reckoning for the life of man" — Hebrew "darash" — to seek, require, demand an accounting. ESV's "require a reckoning" makes the judicial sense clearer.
v.6              | KJV         | "Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed" / — Both equivalent. The institution of capital punishment. The basis: the image of God. To murder a human is to assault God's image.
v.9              | KJV         | "with your seed after you" / ESV: "your offspring after you" — Hebrew "zera" — seed, offspring. ESV's "offspring" is clearer for modern readers.
v.12             | KJV         | "the token of the covenant" / ESV: "the sign of the covenant" — Hebrew "oth" — sign, token, mark. ESV's "sign" is more natural modern English; KJV's "token" is slightly archaic.
v.13             | KJV         | "I do set my bow in the cloud" / ESV: "I have set my bow in the cloud" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "qesheth" — a war bow. God sets His weapon in the sky as a sign of peace. The bow points upward (toward God), not downward (toward earth).
v.20             | KJV         | "Noah began to be an husbandman" / ESV: "Noah began to be a man of the soil" — Hebrew "ish ha'adamah" — a man of the ground/soil. ESV is more literal; KJV's "husbandman" (farmer) is archaic English.
v.22             | KJV         | "saw the nakedness of his father" / ESV: "saw the nakedness of his father" — Both identical. Hebrew "ra'ah eth ervath" — the exact nature of Ham's sin is debated. At minimum: disrespect and exploitation of his father's vulnerability. He publicized what should have been covered.
v.25             | KJV         | "Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants" / ESV: "Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be to his brothers" — Both equivalent. Noah curses Canaan (Ham's son), not Ham directly. This prophecy is fulfilled when Israel conquers Canaan. It is NOT a justification for racial slavery — it refers to specific nations, not races.
v.27             | KJV         | "God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem" / ESV: "May God enlarge Japheth, and let him dwell in the tents of Shem" — Hebrew "yapht" — a wordplay on Japheth's name (enlarge/ make spacious). ESV renders it as a wish/prayer ("May God..."); KJV as a declaration ("God shall..."). Both are valid readings of the Hebrew jussive form. Japheth's descendants will share in Shem's spiritual blessings — fulfilled as Gentiles are included in the Abrahamic covenant through Christ. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.2              | NET         | "are under your authority" — interprets "into your hand are they delivered" (ASV) as a statement of authority/dominion, making the governance concept explicit where KJV/ESV use physical "hand" imagery.
v.12             | NET         | "This is the guarantee of the covenant" — uniquely renders Hebrew "oth" (sign/token) as "guarantee," emphasizing the legal/binding nature of the covenant sign rather than merely visual.
v.13             | WEB         | "I set my rainbow in the cloud" — uses "rainbow" explicitly where KJV/ASV have simply "bow" and ESV "my bow." WEB makes the referent immediately clear for modern readers.
v.26             | NET         | "Worthy of praise is the Lord, the God of Shem!" — an interpretive expansion of "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem" (KJV/ESV), rendering the Hebrew blessing formula as a declaration of God's praiseworthiness rather than a wish for God to be blessed.
v.27             | NET         | "May God enlarge Japheth's territory and numbers!" — adds "territory and numbers" to make explicit what the enlargement entails, going beyond the Hebrew text which simply says "enlarge Japheth."

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The rainbow       | God's covenant sign. A war bow laid down — pointing toward heaven. A visible promise of mercy. Blood prohibition — Blood represents life and belongs to God. Not to be consumed — a principle predating the Mosaic Law. Capital punishment — Human government authorized to execute murderers. Based on the image of God, not mere social contract. Shem, Ham, Japheth — Noah's three sons from whom all nations descend. Shem = Messianic line (Semites). Ham = Canaanites, Africans. Japheth = Europeans, Indo-Europeans. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "I do set my bow in the cloud" (v.13). Every rainbow is God preaching His
   faithfulness. Do you notice? Do you remember His promises when you see
   it?

2. "For in the image of God made he man" (v.6). Every human being bears
   God's image — regardless of race, status, or condition. How does this
   shape how you treat people?

3. Noah — the righteous man who walked with God — got drunk and was shamed
   (vv.20-21). Even the godly can fall. Are you guarding against
   complacency? Past faithfulness doesn't guarantee future obedience.

4. Shem and Japheth covered their father's shame (v.23). When someone you
   respect fails, do you expose them (like Ham) or cover them with grace
   (like Shem and Japheth)?

5. "God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem"
   (v.27). Gentiles sharing in Israel's spiritual blessings — fulfilled in
   Christ. If you're a Gentile believer, you are living in this prophecy.

10.ESV:
1. These are the generations of the sons of Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Sons were born to them after the flood.
2. The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.
3. The sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah.
4. The sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.
5. From these the coastland peoples spread in their lands, each with his own language, by their clans, in their nations.
6. The sons of Ham: Cush, Egypt, Put, and Canaan.
7. The sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabteca. The sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan.
8. Cush fathered Nimrod; he was the first on earth to be a mighty man.
9. He was a mighty hunter before the LORD. Therefore it is said, “Like Nimrod a mighty hunter before the LORD.”
10. The beginning of his kingdom was Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.
11. From that land he went into Assyria and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah, and
12. Resen between Nineveh and Calah; that is the great city.
13. Egypt fathered Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim,
14. Pathrusim, Casluhim (from whom the Philistines came), and Caphtorim.
15. Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn and Heth,
16. and the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites,
17. the Hivites, the Arkites, the Sinites,
18. the Arvadites, the Zemarites, and the Hamathites. Afterward the clans of the Canaanites dispersed.
19. And the territory of the Canaanites extended from Sidon in the direction of Gerar as far as Gaza, and in the direction of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, as far as Lasha.
20. These are the sons of Ham, by their clans, their languages, their lands, and their nations.
21. To Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the elder brother of Japheth, children were born.
22. The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, and Aram.
23. The sons of Aram: Uz, Hul, Gether, and Mash.
24. Arpachshad fathered Shelah; and Shelah fathered Eber.
25. To Eber were born two sons: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided, and his brother’s name was Joktan.
26. Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,
27. Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,
28. Obal, Abimael, Sheba,
29. Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab; all these were the sons of Joktan.
30. The territory in which they lived extended from Mesha in the direction of Sephar to the hill country of the east.
31. These are the sons of Shem, by their clans, their languages, their lands, and their nations.
32. These are the clans of the sons of Noah, according to their genealogies, in their nations, and from these the nations spread abroad on the earth after the flood.

10.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Table of Nations traces Noah's descendants — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — as they spread across the earth after the flood.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 10 is the "Table of Nations" — a genealogical
record of how Noah's three sons populated the earth after the flood. It is
unique in ancient literature: no other ancient document attempts to trace all
nations back to a common ancestor. The chapter lists 70 nations (a number
symbolizing completeness) descended from Shem, Ham, and Japheth. It provides
the backdrop for chapter 11 (Babel) and chapter 12 (Abraham's call from among
the nations).

Japheth's Descendants (vv.2-5): The Indo-European peoples. Settled in Asia
Minor, Europe, and the coastlands. Includes ancestors of Greeks (Javan),
Persians (Madai), Russians/Turks (Meshech, Tubal), and others.

Ham's Descendants (vv.6-20): The African and Canaanite peoples. Includes
Cush (Ethiopia), Mizraim (Egypt), Put (Libya), and Canaan. Notable: Nimrod
(vv.8-12) — "a mighty hunter before the LORD" who founded Babel (Babylon)
and Nineveh. The Canaanite peoples are listed (vv.15-19) — the nations Israel
will later displace.

Shem's Descendants (vv.21-31): The Semitic peoples (Middle Eastern). Includes
Elam (Persia), Asshur (Assyria), Arphaxad (ancestor of Abraham), and Aram
(Syria). Eber (v.24) — ancestor of the "Hebrews." Peleg — "in his days was
the earth divided" (v.25).

Summary (v.32): "By these were the nations divided in the earth after the
flood." All humanity traces back to one family.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• The Table of Nations maps the spread of humanity from Noah's three sons:
  Japheth (Indo-European peoples, Anatolia/Europe), Ham (Africa, Canaan,
  Mesopotamia), and Shem (Semitic peoples, the Near East).
• Nimrod's kingdom includes Babel, Erech (Uruk), Accad, and Calneh in Shinar
  (Babylonia) and Nineveh in Assyria (vv.10-12) — all in modern Iraq.
• This chapter provides the geographical backdrop for the entire Old Testament.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Acts        | 17      | 26                 | "He made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth." (ESV)
v.5              | Deuteronomy | 32      | 8                  | "When the Most High gave to the nations their inheritance, when he divided mankind." (ESV)
v.8-9            | Micah       | 5       | 6                  | "The land of Nimrod." (ESV)
v.25             | Genesis     | 11      | 1-9                | "The earth was divided — Babel and the scattering of nations." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-10/
  Guzik covers the Table of Nations, Nimrod, the three family lines, and
  the theological significance of all nations from one source.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "One Blood" (referencing Acts 17:26 and Genesis 10)
  "God hath made of one blood all nations — all from Noah, all from Adam.
  There is one human race, one human family. The Table of Nations in Genesis
  10 is God's family tree for all humanity. Every person you meet is your
  relative — however distant. This destroys racism at its root: we are all
  one family, all bearing one image, all needing one Savior."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "these are the generations" / ESV: "These are the generations" — Both identical. Hebrew "toledoth" — the fourth toledoth section in Genesis. The Table of Nations.
v.5              | KJV         | "the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands; every one after his tongue, after their families, in their nations" / ESV: "From these the coastland peoples spread in their lands, each with his own language, by their clans, in their nations" — Hebrew "iyyey haggoyim" — the coastlands of the nations. KJV's "isles of the Gentiles" is more archaic; ESV's "coastland peoples" is more precise. KJV's "tongue" = ESV's "language"; KJV's "families" = ESV's "clans." Maritime peoples who spread across the Mediterranean.
v.6              | KJV         | "Mizraim" / ESV: "Egypt" — Hebrew "Mitsrayim." KJV retains the Hebrew name; ESV translates it to the familiar English name. Mizraim is the Hebrew word for Egypt.
v.6              | KJV         | "Phut" / ESV: "Put" — Both are transliterations of the same Hebrew name; ESV uses the more standard modern spelling.
v.8              | KJV         | "he began to be a mighty one in the earth" / ESV: "he was the first on earth to be a mighty man" — Hebrew "gibbor" — a warrior, a powerful man. ESV's "first on earth to be a mighty man" emphasizes Nimrod's pioneering role as the first post-flood empire-builder. "Before the LORD" — could mean "in the LORD's sight" (neutral) or "in defiance of the LORD" (negative).
v.9              | KJV         | "a mighty hunter before the LORD" / ESV: "a mighty hunter before the LORD" — Both identical. Possibly a hunter of men (conqueror) rather than just animals. His kingdom (Babel) suggests political/military power, not just hunting skill.
v.10             | KJV         | "Babel" / ESV: "Babel" — Both identical. Hebrew "Babel" — confusion (cf. 11:9). The origin of Babylon — the city that represents human rebellion against God throughout Scripture.
v.11             | KJV         | "Out of that land went forth Asshur" / ESV: "From that land he went into Assyria" — This is a significant interpretive difference. KJV reads "Asshur" as the subject (a person named Asshur left and built Nineveh); ESV reads Nimrod as the subject who went into Assyria (the land of Asshur). ESV's reading makes Nimrod the builder of both Babel and Nineveh — a greater empire-builder.
v.14             | KJV         | "Philistim" / ESV: "the Philistines" — Both refer to the same people. ESV uses the familiar English name; KJV uses a closer transliteration of the Hebrew.
v.21             | KJV         | "Eber" / ESV: "Eber" — Both identical. Hebrew "Ever" — from which "Hebrew" (Ivri) derives. The ancestor of the Hebrew people.
v.25             | KJV         | "in his days was the earth divided" / ESV: "in his days the earth was divided" — Both identical in meaning. Hebrew "niphlegah ha'arets." Options: (1) the division of languages at Babel (ch.11), (2) a geographical/continental division, (3) political division of peoples.
v.32             | KJV         | "by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood" / ESV: "from these the nations spread abroad on the earth after the flood" — Hebrew "niphredu" — were separated, spread out. ESV's "spread abroad" is more neutral; KJV's "divided" emphasizes the separation. Both convey that all nations trace back to Noah's family. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.8              | NET         | "he began to be a valiant warrior on the earth" — renders Hebrew "gibbor" as "valiant warrior" rather than "mighty one" (KJV/ESV/ ASV/WEB), making Nimrod's military nature explicit from the start.
v.10             | NET         | "The primary regions of his kingdom" — renders Hebrew "reshith mamlakhto" as "primary regions" rather than "beginning of his kingdom" (KJV/ESV/ASV), interpreting "reshith" geographically rather than temporally. - v.11 — WEB/NET both clearly make Nimrod the subject who went into Assyria and built Nineveh — resolving the KJV ambiguity about whether "Asshur" (a person) or Nimrod went forth.
v.5              | NET         | "the coastlands of the nations were separated into their lands" — uses "separated" for the division, emphasizing intentional distribution rather than mere spreading. ASV's "isles of the nations divided" retains the older "isles" (coastlands) terminology.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Nimrod            | The first empire-builder. Founded Babel and Nineveh. A "mighty one" — possibly in defiance of God. | 
Babel/Babylon     | The city founded by Nimrod. Represents human rebellion against God throughout Scripture — from Genesis 10 to Revelation 18. Seventy nations — The traditional count of nations in this chapter. Represents the complete spread of humanity. | 
One blood         | All nations descend from one family (Noah/Adam). The foundation for human unity and the rejection of racism. | 
Shem              | The ancestor of the Semitic peoples and the Messianic line. Abraham, Israel, and Christ descend from Shem. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "By these were the nations divided" (v.32). All humanity comes from one
   family. How does this shape your view of people from other nations,
   cultures, and ethnicities? We are all one family.

2. Nimrod built Babel — the beginning of organized rebellion against God
   (vv.8-10). Where do you see "Babel" today — human systems built in
   defiance of God? Are you participating in them or standing apart?

3. God knows every nation, every family, every person. This chapter shows
   His awareness of all peoples. He is not the God of one nation but of all
   nations. Does your faith reflect this global perspective?

4. From these 70 nations, God will call ONE man (Abraham, ch.12) to bless
   ALL nations. God's plan is always particular (one man, one nation) for
   the sake of the universal (all peoples). How are you part of God's plan
   to bless all nations?

5. The Messianic line runs through Shem (v.21). God is already narrowing the
   focus toward the Redeemer promised in 3:15. Can you trace God's
   faithfulness through the generations?

David Guzik — Genesis 10 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-10/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
