11.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Humanity rebels at Babel, building a tower to make a name for themselves; God confuses their language and scatters them, then traces the line from Shem to Abram.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 11 has two parts: the Tower of Babel (vv.1-9) and
the genealogy from Shem to Abram (vv.10-32). Babel represents humanity's
united rebellion against God — building a tower to "make us a name" rather
than filling the earth as commanded. God responds by confusing languages and
scattering the people. The genealogy then narrows from all nations to one
family — Terah's — setting the stage for Abram's call in chapter 12.

The Tower of Babel (vv.1-9): One language, one people (v.1). They settle in
Shinar (Babylon) and decide to build a city and tower "whose top may reach
unto heaven" (v.4). Their motive: "let us make us a name, lest we be
scattered" (v.4) — directly opposing God's command to fill the earth (9:1).
God "came down" to see their work (v.5 — ironic: their tower reaching heaven
is so small God must come down). God confuses their language (v.7) and
scatters them (v.8). The city is named "Babel" — confusion (v.9).

The Genealogy of Shem (vv.10-26): Ten generations from Shem to Abram.
Lifespans decrease dramatically (Shem 600 → Terah 205). The line narrows
toward the one man through whom God will bless all nations.

Terah's Family (vv.27-32): Terah has three sons: Abram, Nahor, Haran. Haran
dies young in Ur (v.28). Abram marries Sarai — "she was barren" (v.30). Terah
takes the family from Ur toward Canaan but stops at Haran (v.31). Terah dies
in Haran (v.32). The stage is set for God's call.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Shinar (v.2) — the plain of Babylonia (modern southern Iraq). The people
  migrate eastward (away from God's presence, the recurring Genesis pattern).
• Babel (v.9) — Babylon, one of the most significant cities in ancient
  history. Its name means "gate of god" but is linked to "confusion" (balal).
• Ur of the Chaldees (v.28) — Abram's origin city in southern Mesopotamia
  (modern Tell el-Muqayyar, Iraq). A major Sumerian urban center.
• Haran (v.31) — a city in northwest Mesopotamia (modern Harran, Turkey), a
  caravan crossroads on the route between Ur and Canaan.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1-9            | Acts        | 2       | 5-11               | "Pentecost reverses Babel — every nation hears in their own tongue." (ESV)
v.4              | Proverbs    | 16      | 18                 | "Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall." (ESV)
v.7              | Isaiah      | 55      | 8-9                | "My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways." (ESV)
v.31             | Acts        | 7       | 2-4                | "The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham when he was in Mesopotamia." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-11/
  Guzik covers Babel's rebellion, God's response, the genealogy, and the
  transition to Abram.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Babel" on vv.4-9 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 828)
  "Let US make US a name — self-glory, self-determination, self-sufficiency.
  This is the essence of all sin: 'us' instead of God. They wanted to reach
  heaven on their own terms — by their own effort, for their own glory. God's
  response: confusion and scattering. Every attempt to build a kingdom without
  God ends in confusion. Every tower of human pride is brought low."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "one language, and of one speech" / ESV: "one language and the same words" — Hebrew "saphah echath udbarim achadim" — one lip and one set of words. Complete linguistic unity. Both equivalent in meaning; ESV renders "dbarim" more literally as "words."
v.4              | KJV         | "whose top may reach unto heaven" / ESV: "with its top in the heavens" — Not literally reaching heaven but a tower of religious/ political ambition. A ziggurat — a stepped temple-tower common in Mesopotamia. ESV drops the KJV's "may reach" (italicized/supplied in KJV), staying closer to the Hebrew construct.
v.4              | KJV         | "make us a name" / ESV: "let us make a name for ourselves" — Hebrew "na'aseh lanu shem" — make for ourselves a reputation. Both equivalent. Self-glorification rather than glorifying God. Contrast with 12:2 where GOD makes Abram's name great.
v.5              | KJV         | "the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded" / ESV: "the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of man had built" — Anthropomorphic language. Both equivalent. Ironic: they built UP to reach heaven; God came DOWN to see their puny effort.
v.7              | KJV         | "let us go down, and there confound their language" / ESV: "let us go down and there confuse their language" — The divine plural again (cf. 1:26, 3:22). The Trinity in action. ESV modernizes "confound" to "confuse" — same Hebrew "balal."
v.9              | KJV         | "Babel" / ESV: "Babel" — Both identical. Hebrew "balal" = to confuse/mix. A wordplay: the Babylonians claimed "Babel" meant "gate of God" (Bab-ili). God says it means "confusion." KJV: "confound";
v.30             | KJV         | "barren" / ESV: "barren" — Both identical. Hebrew "aqarah" — unable to conceive. A critical detail: God's promise of a great nation (12:2) will require a miracle. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | NET         | "a common language and a common vocabulary" — uniquely renders Hebrew "saphah echath udbarim achadim" by distinguishing between "language" (grammar/system) and "vocabulary" (shared words), where KJV/ESV/ASV combine them as "one language and one speech."
v.3              | WEB         | "they used tar for mortar" — renders Hebrew "chemar" as "tar" where KJV/ASV have "slime" (archaic) and ESV has "bitumen." WEB's "tar" is the most immediately accessible for modern readers.
v.4              | WEB         | "a tower whose top reaches to the sky" — uses "sky" where KJV/ESV/ASV have "heaven(s)," subtly distinguishing physical height from spiritual aspiration.
v.6              | NET         | "nothing they plan to do will be beyond them" — a more dynamic interpretation than "nothing will be withholden/restrained from them" (KJV/ESV), emphasizing limitless human potential when unified in purpose.
v.28             | NET         | "while his father Terah was still alive" — adds this interpretive note (not explicit in the Hebrew) to clarify the tragedy of Haran dying before his father.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
God came down     | Ironic divine descent. Humanity's greatest achievement is so small that God must stoop to see it. Sarai was barren — The humanly impossible situation that sets up God's miraculous intervention. God works through impossibility. Ur of the Chaldees — Abram's hometown in southern Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). A sophisticated pagan city. God calls Abram OUT of paganism. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Let us make us a name" (v.4). Where are you building for YOUR name
   rather than God's? Self-glory is the essence of Babel. Are you building a
   tower of reputation, achievement, or control?

2. "The LORD came down to see" (v.5). Your greatest achievements are tiny
   from God's perspective. Does this humble you? Does it free you from the
   pressure to impress?

3. God scattered what humanity united in rebellion (v.8). But at Pentecost
   (Acts 2), God united what was scattered — through the gospel. The cure
   for Babel is not human unity but Spirit-created unity in Christ.

4. "Sarai was barren" (v.30). God's greatest works often begin with
   impossibility. Where in your life does the situation seem impossible?
   That may be exactly where God plans to work.

5. Terah started toward Canaan but stopped at Haran (v.31). He got halfway
   and settled. Are you settling for halfway obedience? God's call requires
   going all the way.

12.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God calls Abram to leave everything for an unknown land, promising to make him a great nation and bless all families through him.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 12 is one of the most pivotal chapters in the
Bible — the call of Abram and the Abrahamic Covenant. After 11 chapters of
universal history (creation, fall, flood, Babel), God now narrows His focus
to one man from whom He will build a nation, and through that nation bless
ALL nations. This is the beginning of redemptive history proper — God's plan
to reverse the curse of Genesis 3 through a chosen people. Abram is called
from Ur/Haran (pagan Mesopotamia) to Canaan (the Promised Land) around
2091 BC.

The Call and Promise (vv.1-3): God commands Abram to leave everything — country,
kindred, father's house (v.1). Seven promises: (1) I will make of thee a great
nation, (2) I will bless thee, (3) make thy name great, (4) thou shalt be a
blessing, (5) I will bless them that bless thee, (6) curse him that curseth
thee, (7) in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed (vv.2-3).

Abram's Obedience (vv.4-9): "So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken" (v.4).
Age 75. He takes Sarai, Lot, and possessions to Canaan (vv.4-5). At Shechem,
God appears: "Unto thy seed will I give this land" (v.7). Abram builds altars
at Shechem and Bethel (vv.7-8).

Abram's Failure in Egypt (vv.10-20): Famine drives Abram to Egypt (v.10). He
lies about Sarai — "Say... thou art my sister" (v.13). Pharaoh takes Sarai;
God plagues Pharaoh (vv.15-17). Pharaoh rebukes Abram and sends him away
(vv.18-20). Even the father of faith stumbles — but God protects His promise.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Abram's route: Ur → Haran → Shechem → Bethel → Negev → Egypt. This traces
  the entire Fertile Crescent and into North Africa.
• Shechem (v.6) — the first named place in Canaan where God appears to Abram.
  Located in the central hill country between Mount Ebal and Gerizim.
• Bethel (v.8) — meaning "house of God," between Bethel and Ai in the central
  highlands. A recurring worship site throughout Genesis.
• Egypt (v.10) — Abram goes south due to famine; Egypt's Nile-fed agriculture
  made it a refuge during Canaanite droughts.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1-3            | Galatians   | 3       | 8                  | "The Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham." (ESV)
v.2              | Romans      | 4       | 13                 | "The promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world." (ESV)
v.3              | Acts        | 3       | 25                 | "In your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed." (ESV)
v.3              | Galatians   | 3       | 14                 | "So that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles." (ESV)
v.7              | Romans      | 4       | 13                 | "He would be heir of the world." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-12/
  Guzik covers the call, the sevenfold promise, Abram's obedience, and the
  Egypt failure.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Call of Abraham" on vv.1-3 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 833)
  "Get thee OUT — leave. From thy country — the familiar. From thy kindred —
  the comfortable. From thy father's house — the secure. Unto a land that I
  WILL SHEW thee — not 'that I have shown' but 'will show.' He must leave
  before he sees. He must obey before he understands. This is faith: obeying
  God's command before seeing God's destination. And the promise: I WILL bless,
  I WILL make great, ALL families blessed. God's 'I will' is enough."
  Sermon: "In Thee Shall All Families Be Blessed" on v.3 (MTP Vol. 20)
  "ALL families of the earth — not just Israel, not just the Middle East —
  all. This is the gospel in Genesis: through one man's seed (Christ), every
  nation will have access to blessing. The Abrahamic covenant is the
  foundation of world missions."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "Get thee out" / ESV: "Go from your country" — Hebrew "lek leka" — go for yourself, go for your own benefit. God's command is also for Abram's good. KJV's archaic "get thee out" and ESV's "go from" both capture the imperative; ESV loses the reflexive nuance of "leka" (for yourself). v.1  — Three levels of separation: country (nation), kindred (extended family), father's house (immediate family). Each more costly than the last. Both translations identical in structure.
v.2              | KJV         | "make thy name great" / ESV: "make your name great" — Both equivalent. Contrast with Babel: "let us make us a name" (11:4). At Babel, humans tried to make their own name; God makes Abram's name great. Self-exaltation fails; God-exaltation succeeds.
v.3              | KJV         | "all families of the earth" / ESV: "all the families of the earth shall be blessed" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "kol mishpechoth ha'adamah" — every clan/family of the ground. Universal scope. The Abrahamic blessing is for everyone.
v.3              | KJV         | "blessed" / ESV: "blessed" — Both identical. Hebrew "nibrechu" — a form that can mean "bless themselves" (reflexive) or "be blessed" (passive). Either way: Abram's seed brings blessing to all peoples. Fulfilled in Christ (Gal 3:16).
v.7              | KJV         | "unto thy seed" / ESV: "To your offspring" — Hebrew "lezar'aka" — to your offspring/descendant. ESV uses "offspring" (modern English); KJV uses "seed" (closer to Hebrew "zera"). Both collective (Israel) and singular (Christ, Gal 3:16).
v.13             | KJV         | "thou art my sister" / ESV: "you are my sister" — Both equivalent. A half-truth (Sarai was his half-sister, 20:12). But deceptive in intent. Fear displaced faith. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | WEB         | "Leave your country, and your relatives" — renders Hebrew "moladteka" as "relatives" rather than "kindred" (KJV/ASV). NET uses "your relatives" as well, modernizing the language.
v.2              | NET         | "so that you will exemplify divine blessing" — a uniquely interpretive rendering of Hebrew "heyeh berakah" (KJV: "be thou a blessing" / ESV: "be a blessing"). NET makes Abram a visible model of what it looks like to be blessed by God.
v.3              | NET         | "the one who treats you lightly I must curse" — renders Hebrew "meqallelka" as "treats you lightly" (vs. "curseth" KJV / "dishonors" ESV), capturing the root meaning of "qalal" (to make light/trivial).
v.9              | NET         | "Abram continually journeyed by stages down to the Negev" — adds "by stages" to render the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction, showing the gradual, progressive nature of the journey.
v.20             | NET         | "they expelled him" — a harsher reading than "sent him away" (KJV/ESV) or "escorted him away" (WEB), emphasizing Pharaoh's anger.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Altars            | Abram's response to God's appearances: worship. He marks his journey with altars — places of encounter and devotion. The Egypt failure — Abram's lapse of faith: lying about Sarai to protect himself. Even the father of faith stumbles — but God protects His promise. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Get thee out... unto a land that I will shew thee" (v.1). God called
   Abram to leave before showing the destination. Has God ever called you to
   step out without knowing where you're going? Faith means obeying before
   seeing.

2. "In thee shall all families of the earth be blessed" (v.3). You are part
   of this promise's fulfillment. Are you a blessing to the nations? How are
   you participating in God's plan to bless all peoples?

3. "So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken" (v.4). Simple, immediate
   obedience. When God speaks, do you depart? Or do you negotiate, delay,
   and make excuses?

4. Abram built altars wherever he went (vv.7-8). He marked his journey with
   worship. Do you mark your life with worship — in every new place, every
   new season?

5. Abram's Egypt failure (vv.10-20) shows that even great faith has weak
   moments. Fear displaced trust. Where does fear cause you to compromise
   your integrity? God protected His promise despite Abram's failure — He'll
   protect His purposes in your life too.

  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 12 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-12/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

13.ESV:
1. So Abram went up from Egypt, he and his wife and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the Negeb.
2. Now Abram was very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold.
3. And he journeyed on from the Negeb as far as Bethel to the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Ai,
4. to the place where he had made an altar at the first. And there Abram called upon the name of the LORD.
5. And Lot, who went with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents,
6. so that the land could not support both of them dwelling together; for their possessions were so great that they could not dwell together,
7. and there was strife between the herdsmen of Abram’s livestock and the herdsmen of Lot’s livestock. At that time the Canaanites and the Perizzites were dwelling in the land.
8. Then Abram said to Lot, “Let there be no strife between you and me, and between your herdsmen and my herdsmen, for we are kinsmen.

16. I will make your offspring as the dust of the earth, so that if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted.

13.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 13
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Abram and Lot separate due to their growing wealth; Lot chooses Sodom's fertile plain while God reaffirms His promise to Abram.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 13 records Abram's return from Egypt, his
separation from Lot, and God's reaffirmation of the land promise. After the
failure of chapter 12 (Egypt), Abram returns to the altar at Bethel — back to
the place of worship and faith. The separation from Lot demonstrates Abram's
generous, faith-filled character: he gives Lot first choice, trusting God to
provide. Lot chooses by sight; Abram walks by faith.

Return to Bethel (vv.1-4): Abram returns from Egypt — wealthy (v.2) — to
Bethel, "unto the place of the altar" (v.4). He "called on the name of the
LORD" (v.4). After failure, he returns to worship. Restoration.

The Separation (vv.5-13): Both Abram and Lot are too wealthy to share the
land (vv.5-6). Strife between their herdsmen (v.7). Abram offers Lot first
choice: "Is not the whole land before thee?" (v.9). Lot "lifted up his eyes"
and chose the well-watered Jordan plain — "even as the garden of the LORD"
(v.10). He "pitched his tent toward Sodom" (v.12). Ominous note: "the men of
Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly" (v.13).

God's Promise Reaffirmed (vv.14-18): After Lot leaves, God tells Abram:
"Lift up now thine eyes" (v.14) — contrast with Lot lifting his eyes (v.10).
Lot looked and chose for himself; God tells Abram to look and receive from
Him. "All the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed
for ever" (v.15). "Thy seed as the dust of the earth" (v.16). "Arise, walk
through the land" (v.17). Abram settles at Mamre/Hebron and builds another
altar (v.18).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Bethel (v.4) — Abram returns to where he built an altar, re-establishing
  worship after the Egypt detour. Located in the central hill country.
• The Jordan plain (v.10) — described as "well watered every where... even as
  the garden of the LORD" — an Eden-like paradise. Lot chooses it for its
  appearance, not its righteousness.
• Sodom and Gomorrah (v.10) — located in the Jordan plain, likely near the
  southern end of the Dead Sea. Choosing beauty over godliness.
• Hebron/Mamre (v.18) — Abram settles here after Lot departs. Located in the
  southern hill country, about 19 miles south of Jerusalem.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.14-17          | Romans      | 4       | 13                 | "The promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world." (ESV)
v.15             | Galatians   | 3       | 16                 | "To your offspring, who is Christ." (ESV)
v.16             | Romans      | 4       | 18                 | "So shall your offspring be." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-13/
  Guzik covers the return to Bethel, the generous separation, Lot's
  dangerous choice, and God's expanded promise.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Lot's Choice" on vv.10-12 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 636)
  "Lot lifted up HIS eyes — he looked for himself. He beheld the well-watered
  plain — he chose by appearance. He pitched his tent TOWARD Sodom — he moved
  in the wrong direction. This is the anatomy of backsliding: self-directed
  vision, appearance-based decisions, gradual movement toward evil. First
  toward Sodom, then in Sodom (14:12), then at Sodom's gate (19:1). It starts
  with a glance and ends with captivity."
  Sermon: "Abram's Generosity" on vv.8-9 (MTP Vol. 20, No. 1198)
  "Let there be no strife — Abram could have pulled rank. He was the elder,
  the one with the promise, the one God called. He had every right to choose
  first. But he gave that right away. Why? Because he trusted God. When you
  trust God to provide, you don't need to grab. When you know God has your
  future, you can afford to be generous with your present."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.2              | KJV         | "very rich" / ESV: "very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "kabed meod" — very heavy/wealthy. In cattle, silver, and gold. Wealth is not condemned — but how you use it and what you trust matters.
v.9              | KJV         | "separate thyself" / ESV: "Separate yourself from me" — Both equivalent. Abram initiates the solution and gives Lot the advantage. Generosity born of faith — he trusts God, not circumstances.
v.10             | KJV         | "Lot lifted up his eyes" / ESV: "Lot lifted up his eyes" — Both identical. He chose by what he SAW — the appearance of prosperity. He didn't consult God. He chose by sight, not faith.
v.12             | KJV         | "pitched his tent toward Sodom" / ESV: "moved his tent as far as Sodom" — Hebrew "ad Sedom" — as far as Sodom. ESV renders the Hebrew more literally ("as far as"); KJV's "toward" captures the directional/spiritual drift. The direction of his tent reveals the direction of his heart. Gradual drift toward evil.
v.14             | KJV         | "Lift up now thine eyes" / ESV: "Lift up your eyes and look" — Both equivalent. God tells Abram to look AFTER Lot has chosen. Lot chose for himself; God chooses for Abram. God's choice is always better.
v.16             | KJV         | "as the dust of the earth" / ESV: "as the dust of the earth" — Both identical. Innumerable. Later: "as the stars" (15:5) and "as the sand" (22:17). Three images of countless descendants.
v.18             | KJV         | "built there an altar" / ESV: "he built an altar to the LORD" — Both equivalent. Abram's fourth altar. His life is marked by worship at every significant location. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.10             | NET         | "He noticed that all of it was well watered...like the garden of the Lord" — adds "He noticed" to make Lot's assessment process explicit. makes the Eden-echo more theologically pointed.
v.13             | NET         | "extremely wicked rebels against the Lord" — intensifies the Hebrew description beyond "wicked and sinners" (KJV/ESV) by adding "rebels," interpreting their sin as active defiance against God.
v.8              | WEB         | "we are relatives" — renders Hebrew "achim" as "relatives" rather than "brethren" (KJV/ASV), a more precise rendering since they were uncle and nephew, not brothers.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Return to the altar | Restoration after failure. Going back to the place of worship and recommitting to God. | 
"Let there be no strife" | Abram's generous peacemaking. Trusting God enough to give others first choice.    | KJV term
Lifted up his eyes | Lot's self-directed choice based on appearance. Choosing by sight rather than faith. | 
Pitched his tent toward Sodom | The first step of gradual drift toward evil. Direction reveals destination.       | 
"Lift up now thine eyes" | God's invitation to Abram to see what GOD gives (not what man chooses). God's provision exceeds human choice. | 
Dust of the earth | Image of innumerable descendants. God's promise of abundance beyond counting.     | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Unto the place of the altar" (v.4). After failure (Egypt), Abram
   returned to worship. When you fail, do you return to God? Or do you stay
   away in shame? Go back to the altar.

2. "Let there be no strife" (v.8). Abram chose peace over rights. Are you
   willing to give up your rights for the sake of peace? Can you trust God
   enough to let others choose first?

3. "Lot lifted up his eyes" (v.10). Lot chose by appearance — and it led to
   disaster. Where are you making decisions based on what LOOKS good rather
   than what IS good? Are you consulting God or just your eyes?

4. "Pitched his tent toward Sodom" (v.12). What direction is your "tent"
   facing? What are you moving toward — gradually, imperceptibly? Direction
   determines destination.

5. God's promise to Abram (vv.14-17) came AFTER he gave generously to Lot.
   You cannot out-give God. When you release with open hands, God fills them
   with more than you released. Are you holding tightly or giving freely?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 13 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-13/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

14.ESV:
1. In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim,
2. these kings made war with Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar).
3. And all these joined forces in the Valley of Siddim (that is, the Salt Sea).
4. Twelve years they had served Chedorlaomer, but in the thirteenth year they rebelled.
5. In the fourteenth year Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him came and defeated the Rephaim in Ashteroth-karnaim, the Zuzim in Ham, the Emim in Shaveh-kiriathaim,
6. and the Horites in their hill country of Seir as far as El-paran on the border of the wilderness.
7. Then they turned back and came to En-mishpat (that is, Kadesh) and defeated all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites who were dwelling in Hazazon-tamar.
8. Then the king of Sodom, the king of Gomorrah, the king of Admah, the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar) went out, and they joined battle in the Valley of Siddim
9. with Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar, four kings against five.
10. Now the Valley of Siddim was full of bitumen pits, and as the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, some fell into them, and the rest fled to the hill country.
11. So the enemy took all the possessions of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their provisions, and went their way.
12. They also took Lot, the son of Abram’s brother, who was dwelling in Sodom, and his possessions, and went their way.
13. Then one who had escaped came and told Abram the Hebrew, who was living by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, brother of Eshcol and of Aner. These were allies of Abram.
14. When Abram heard that his kinsman had been taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, 318 of them, and went in pursuit as far as Dan.
15. And he divided his forces against them by night, he and his servants, and defeated them and pursued them to Hobah, north of Damascus.
16. Then he brought back all the possessions, and also brought back his kinsman Lot with his possessions, and the women and the people.
17. After his return from the defeat of Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him at the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King’s Valley).
18. And Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine. (He was priest of God Most High.)
19. And he blessed him and said, “Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth;
20. and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand!” And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.
21. And the king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the persons, but take the goods for yourself.”
22. But Abram said to the king of Sodom, “I have lifted my hand to the LORD, God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth,
23. that I would not take a thread or a sandal strap or anything that is yours, lest you should say, ‘I have made Abram rich.’
24. I will take nothing but what the young men have eaten, and the share of the men who went with me. Let Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre take their share.”

13. And there came one that had escaped, and told Abram the Hebrew: now he dwelt by the oaks of Mamre, the Amorite, brother of Eshcol, and brother of Aner; and these were confederate with Abram.
14. And when Abram heard that his brother was taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued as far as Dan.
15. And he divided himself against them by night, he and his servants, and smote them, and pursued them unto Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus.
16. And he brought back all the goods, and also brought back his brother Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the people.
17. And the king of Sodom went out to meet him, after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer and the kings that were with him, at the vale of Shaveh (the same is the King`s Vale).
18. And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was priest of God Most High.
19. And he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth:
20. and blessed be God Most High, who hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And he gave him a tenth of all.
21. And the king of Sodom said unto Abram, Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself.
22. And Abram said to the king of Sodom, I have lifted up my hand unto Jehovah, God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth,
23. that I will not take a thread nor a shoe-latchet nor aught that is thine, lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich:
24. save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men that went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their portion.
14.NET:
1. At that time Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Kedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of nations
2. went to war against Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboyim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar).
3. These last five kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim (that is, the Salt Sea).
4. For twelve years they had served Kedorlaomer, but in the thirteenth year they rebelled.
5. In the fourteenth year, Kedorlaomer and the kings who were his allies came and defeated the Rephaites in Ashteroth Karnaim, the Zuzites in Ham, the Emites in Shaveh Kiriathaim,
6. and the Horites in their hill country of Seir, as far as El Paran, which is near the desert.
7. Then they attacked En Mishpat (that is, Kadesh) again, and they conquered all the territory of the Amalekites, as well as the Amorites who were living in Hazezon Tamar.
8. Then the king of Sodom, the king of Gomorrah, the king of Admah, the king of Zeboyim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar) went out and prepared for battle. In the Valley of Siddim they met
9. Kedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of nations, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar. Four kings fought against five.
10. Now the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits. When the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, they fell into them, but some survivors fled to the hills.
11. The four victorious kings took all the possessions and food of Sodom and Gomorrah and left.
12. They also took Abram’s nephew Lot and his possessions when they left, for Lot was living in Sodom.
13. A fugitive came and told Abram the Hebrew. Now Abram was living by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, the brother of Eshcol and Aner. (All these were allied by treaty with Abram.)
14. When Abram heard that his nephew had been taken captive, he mobilized his 318 trained men who had been born in his household, and he pursued the invaders as far as Dan.
15. Then, during the night, Abram divided his forces against them and defeated them. He chased them as far as Hobah, which is north of Damascus.
16. He retrieved all the stolen property. He also brought back his nephew Lot and his possessions, as well as the women and the rest of the people.
17. After Abram returned from defeating Kedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet Abram in the Valley of Shaveh (known as the King’s Valley).
18. Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine. (Now he was the priest of the Most High God.)
19. He blessed Abram, saying, “Blessed be Abram by the Most High God, Creator of heaven and earth.
20. Worthy of praise is the Most High God, who delivered your enemies into your hand.” Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
21. Then the king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the people and take the possessions for yourself.”
22. But Abram replied to the king of Sodom, “I raise my hand to the Lord, the Most High God, Creator of heaven and earth, and vow
23. that I will take nothing belonging to you, not even a thread or the strap of a sandal. That way you can never say, ‘It is I who made Abram rich.’
24. I will take nothing except compensation for what the young men have eaten. As for the share of the men who went with me—Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre—let them take their share.”

24. I will accept nothing from you except that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their portion.”
14.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 14
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Abram rescues Lot from a coalition of kings, then receives a blessing from the mysterious priest-king Melchizedek.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 14 is the first battle narrative in Scripture and
introduces one of the Bible's most mysterious figures: Melchizedek. Four
eastern kings raid the Jordan plain, capturing Lot (who now lives IN Sodom,
v.12 — he's moved from "toward" Sodom in 13:12). Abram rescues Lot with 318
trained servants, then encounters two kings: Melchizedek (who blesses him) and
the king of Sodom (whose offer he refuses). The chapter reveals Abram as
warrior, rescuer, worshipper, and man of integrity.

The War of the Kings (vv.1-12): Four eastern kings (led by Chedorlaomer of
Elam) defeat five kings of the Jordan plain (including Sodom and Gomorrah).
They plunder the cities and capture Lot (v.12).

Abram's Rescue (vv.13-16): Abram hears of Lot's capture (v.13). He arms 318
trained servants (v.14). Night attack — pursues the enemy to Dan and beyond
Damascus (vv.14-15). Recovers everything: goods, people, and Lot (v.16).

Melchizedek (vv.18-20): "King of Salem, priest of the most high God" (v.18).
Brings bread and wine (v.18). Blesses Abram (v.19). Abram gives him a tithe
of everything (v.20). A type of Christ: king-priest, no recorded genealogy,
blesses God's people (cf. Hebrews 7).

The King of Sodom's Offer (vv.21-24): "Give me the persons, and take the
goods" (v.21). Abram refuses everything — "lest thou shouldest say, I have
made Abram rich" (v.23). God alone will be his provider.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• The five cities of the plain: Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Zoar
  (v.2) — all near the southern Dead Sea region.
• The invading kings come from Shinar (Babylonia), Ellasar, Elam (modern
  Iran), and Goiim — a coalition from the east.
• Dan (v.14) — the northernmost extent of the land, showing Abram's military
  reach. Located at the foot of Mount Hermon.
• Salem (v.18) — identified with Jerusalem (Psalm 76:2). Melchizedek rules
  here. First biblical mention of this city.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.18-20          | Hebrews     | 7       | 1-3                | "Melchizedek, king of Salem, priest of the Most High God." (ESV)
v.19             | Psalm       | 110     | 4                  | "You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek." (ESV)
v.22             | Daniel      | 4       | 34                 | "I blessed the Most High, and praised and honored him who lives forever." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-14/
  Guzik covers the war, Abram's rescue, Melchizedek's significance, and
  Abram's refusal of Sodom's wealth.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Melchizedek" on vv.18-20 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 638)
  "King of Salem — king of peace. Priest of the most high God — before Aaron,
  before Levi, before the Law. He appears without genealogy, without beginning
  or end of days — a type of Christ who is priest forever. He blesses Abram
  — the greater blesses the lesser. And Abram tithes to him — acknowledging
  his superiority. In this mysterious figure, we see Christ: king and priest,
  eternal, blessing His people, receiving their worship."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.13             | KJV         | "Abram the Hebrew" / ESV: "Abram the Hebrew" — Both identical. Hebrew "ha-Ivri" — the one from beyond (the river). First use of "Hebrew" in Scripture. Identifies Abram as a foreigner — from beyond the Euphrates.
v.18             | KJV         | "Melchizedek" / ESV: "Melchizedek" — Both identical. Hebrew "Malki-tsedeq" — "my king is righteousness" or "king of righteousness." "Salem" — Hebrew "Shalem" — peace. Later identified with Jerusalem (Psalm 76:2).
v.18             | KJV         | "priest of the most high God" / ESV: "priest of God Most High" — Both equivalent; ESV capitalizes "Most High" as a title. Hebrew "El Elyon" — God Most High. The supreme God above all. Melchizedek served the true God before Israel existed.
v.19             | KJV         | "possessor of heaven and earth" / ESV: "Possessor of heaven and earth" — Both identical. Hebrew "qoneh shamayim va'arets" — creator/ owner of heaven and earth. God owns everything — the basis for Abram's refusal of Sodom's goods.
v.20             | KJV         | "tithes" / ESV: "a tenth of everything" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "ma'aser" — a tenth. ESV makes the meaning explicit. Abram voluntarily gives a tenth to Melchizedek — predating the Mosaic tithe law by centuries.
v.22             | KJV         | "I have lift up mine hand" / ESV: "I have lifted my hand" — Both equivalent. A gesture of oath-taking. Abram swore to God that he would take nothing from Sodom. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | WEB         | "Tidal, king of Goiim" — retains Hebrew "Goiim" (nations) where NET renders it as "nations" directly. ASV also has "Goiim."
v.13             | NET         | "allied by treaty with Abram" — interprets "ba'aley berith" (KJV: "confederate with Abram" / ASV: same) as a formal treaty alliance, making the political arrangement explicit.
v.14             | NET         | "mobilized his 318 trained men" — "mobilized" captures the military urgency better than "armed" (KJV) or "led out" (WEB/ASV).
v.18             | WEB         | "priest of God Most High" — uses "God Most High" (El Elyon) throughout, keeping the Hebrew divine title transparent.
v.19             | NET         | "Creator of heaven and earth" — boldly interprets Hebrew "qoneh" as "Creator" where KJV/ASV have "possessor" and WEB has "possessor." The Hebrew can mean either "possessor/owner" or "creator."

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Melchizedek       | King of Salem, priest of God Most High. A type of Christ: king-priest without recorded genealogy. Blesses Abram and receives his tithe. | 
El Elyon          | "God Most High." The supreme God, possessor of heaven and earth. Above all other claimed deities. | 
Tithe             | A tenth given voluntarily to God's priest. Abram's tithe predates the Mosaic Law — it's a response of worship. | 
King of Salem     | King of peace. Salem = Jerusalem. Melchizedek ruled the city that would become Israel's capital and temple site. Abram the Hebrew — "The one from beyond." Abram identified as a foreigner — from beyond the Euphrates. A stranger in Canaan. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Lot's choice (13:12) led to captivity (14:12). Bad decisions have
   consequences. Where might your "pitching toward Sodom" lead you?

2. Abram risked everything to rescue Lot — despite Lot's foolish choices.
   Are you willing to help those who've made bad decisions? Grace doesn't
   wait for people to deserve rescue.

3. Melchizedek appears with bread and wine — blessing and refreshment after
   battle. Christ meets you after your struggles with sustenance and
   blessing. Are you receiving from Him?

4. Abram refused Sodom's wealth (vv.22-23). He wouldn't let a pagan king
   take credit for God's provision. Where are you tempted to accept
   provision from sources that would compromise your testimony?

5. "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward" (15:1 — immediately
   after this refusal). When you refuse worldly wealth for God's sake, God
   Himself becomes your reward. Is He enough?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 14 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-14/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

15.ESV:
1. After these things the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision: “Fear not, Abram, I am your shield; your reward shall be very great.”
2. But Abram said, “O Lord GOD, what will you give me, for I continue childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus?”
3. And Abram said, “Behold, you have given me no offspring, and a member of my household will be my heir.”
4. And behold, the word of the LORD came to him: “This man shall not be your heir; your very own son shall be your heir.”
5. And he brought him outside and said, “Look toward heaven, and number the stars, if you are able to number them.” Then he said to him, “So shall your offspring be.”
6. And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.
7. And he said to him, “I am the LORD who brought you out from Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land to possess.”
8. But he said, “O Lord GOD, how am I to know that I shall possess it?”
9. He said to him, “Bring me a heifer three years old, a female goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon.”
10. And he brought him all these, cut them in half, and laid each half over against the other. But he did not cut the birds in half.
11. And when birds of prey came down on the carcasses, Abram drove them away.
12. As the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell on Abram. And behold, dreadful and great darkness fell upon him.
13. Then the LORD said to Abram, “Know for certain that your offspring will be sojourners in a land that is not theirs and will be servants there, and they will be afflicted for four hundred years.
14. But I will bring judgment on the nation that they serve, and afterward they shall come out with great possessions.
15. As for you, you shall go to your fathers in peace; you shall be buried in a good old age.
16. And they shall come back here in the fourth generation, for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete.”
17. When the sun had gone down and it was dark, behold, a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch passed between these pieces.
18. On that day the LORD made a covenant with Abram, saying, “To your offspring I give this land, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates,
19. the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
20. the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,
21. the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites and the Jebusites.”

4. Behold, Yahweh’s word came to him, saying, “This man will not be your heir, but he who will come out of your own body will be your heir.”

9. He said to him, “Bring me a heifer three years old, a female goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon.”

14. I will also judge that nation, whom they will serve. Afterward they will come out with great wealth,
15. but you will go to your fathers in peace. You will be buried at a good old age.
16. In the fourth generation they will come here again, for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full.”
17. It came to pass that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold, a smoking furnace, and a flaming torch passed between these pieces.
18. In that day Yahweh made a covenant with Abram, saying, “I have given this land to your offspring, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates:
19. the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
20. the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,
21. the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites.”
15.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 15
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God cuts a covenant with Abram, promising innumerable descendants and the land, and Abram believes — counted to him as righteousness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 15 is one of the most theologically significant
chapters in the OT. It contains: (1) God's self-revelation as Abram's shield
and reward (v.1), (2) the promise of a biological heir (v.4), (3) Abram's
faith counted as righteousness (v.6) — the foundational text for justification
by faith, and (4) the covenant ceremony where God alone passes between the
pieces (vv.9-21) — making the covenant unconditional. This chapter is quoted
extensively by Paul in Romans 4 and Galatians 3.

God's Promise and Abram's Faith (vv.1-6): "Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield,
and thy exceeding great reward" (v.1). Abram's concern: he's still childless
(vv.2-3). God promises a biological heir (v.4). "Look now toward heaven, and
tell the stars... so shall thy seed be" (v.5). "And he believed in the LORD;
and he counted it to him for righteousness" (v.6) — THE verse on justification
by faith.

The Covenant Ceremony (vv.7-21): Abram asks: "Whereby shall I know?" (v.8).
God commands a sacrifice: animals cut in half, pieces arranged (vv.9-10). A
deep sleep and "horror of great darkness" falls on Abram (v.12). God reveals
the future: 400 years of slavery, then deliverance (vv.13-14). A smoking
furnace and burning lamp pass between the pieces (v.17) — GOD ALONE walks
through, making this a unilateral, unconditional covenant. The land promise
defined: from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates (v.18).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• "From the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates" (v.18) —
  the boundaries of the Promised Land defined. The "river of Egypt" is likely
  the Wadi el-Arish (not the Nile), marking the southwestern border.
• The Euphrates marks the northeastern boundary — an enormous territory that
  Israel only briefly controlled under Solomon (1 Kings 4:21).
• Ten nations listed (vv.19-21): Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites,
  Perizzites, Rephaims, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, Jebusites — all
  occupying the land between these boundaries.

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 84      | 11                 | "The LORD God is a sun and shield; the LORD bestows favor and honor." (ESV)
v.5              | Romans      | 4       | 18                 | "In hope he believed against hope, that he should become the father of many nations." (ESV)
v.6              | Romans      | 4       | 3                  | "Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness." (ESV)
v.6              | Galatians   | 3       | 6                  | "Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness." (ESV)
v.13-14          | Acts        | 7       | 6-7                | "His offspring would be sojourners in a land belonging to others." (ESV)
v.17             | Hebrews     | 6       | 13-18              | "When God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-15/
  Guzik covers God as shield/reward, the star promise, justification by
  faith, the covenant ceremony, and the land promise.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Abram's Faith Counted for Righteousness" on v.6 (MTP Vol. 14)
  "He BELIEVED — not worked, not earned, not achieved — believed. In the LORD
  — not in himself, not in circumstances, not in probability — in the LORD.
  And HE (God) counted IT (faith) to HIM (Abram) for RIGHTEOUSNESS. This is
  the gospel before the gospel: righteousness credited to the account of one
  who simply believes God's promise. Not righteousness earned but
  righteousness received. Not works rewarded but faith credited. This is how
  every sinner is justified — by believing God."
  Sermon: "The Smoking Furnace and Burning Lamp" on v.17 (MTP Vol. 9, No. 528)
  "God ALONE passed between the pieces. In ancient covenants, both parties
  walked through — pledging 'may I be cut in pieces if I break this covenant.'
  But here, Abram sleeps. Only God walks through. This covenant depends on
  God alone. If it fails, GOD is cut in pieces — not Abram. And at the cross,
  God WAS 'cut' — Christ's body broken to keep the covenant Abram could never
  keep. Unconditional. Unbreakable. God's alone to fulfill."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.1              | KJV         | "the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision" / ESV: "the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision" — Both equivalent. First use of "the word of the LORD came" — a prophetic formula used hundreds of times in the OT.
v.1              | KJV         | "thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward" / ESV: "your shield; your reward shall be very great" — Hebrew "magen" — a defensive shield. "Exceeding great reward" — Hebrew "sekarekha harbeh meod" — your reward is very great. KJV reads God Himself as the reward; ESV's phrasing could suggest the reward is separate. Hebrew supports both readings.
v.6              | KJV         | "believed" / ESV: "believed" — Both identical. Hebrew "he'emin" — trusted, relied upon, had faith in. From the root "aman" (firm, sure — root of "Amen"). KJV: "counted it to him for righteousness" / "chashab" — reckoned, credited, imputed. An accounting term: faith deposited to Abram's account as righteousness.
v.9              | KJV         | "Take me an heifer of three years old" / ESV: "Bring me a heifer three years old" — Both equivalent. The covenant ceremony: animals cut in half, arranged in two rows. Both parties would walk between them, saying: "May I become like these animals if I break this covenant." A self-maledictory oath.
v.12             | KJV         | "horror of great darkness" / ESV: "dreadful and great darkness" — Both equivalent. Hebrew "emah chashekhah gedolah" — a dreadful, great darkness. The weight of what God is about to reveal and commit to.
v.17             | KJV         | "smoking furnace, and a burning lamp" / ESV: "a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch" — Hebrew "tannur ashan" (smoking oven/ furnace) and "lappid esh" (torch of fire). ESV's "fire pot" and "flaming torch" are more precise modern renderings. Theophany: God's presence as fire and smoke (cf. Exodus 13:21, 19:18). GOD ALONE passes through — making this a unilateral covenant.
v.18             | KJV         | "from the river of Egypt unto the great river" / ESV: "from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates" — Both equivalent; ESV explicitly names the Euphrates. The full extent of the Promised Land. Never fully possessed except briefly under Solomon (1 Kings 4:21). TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.1              | NET         | "the Lord's message came to Abram in a vision" — renders Hebrew "debar Yahweh" as "the Lord's message" rather than "the word of the LORD" (KJV/ESV), emphasizing communication content over divine title.
v.2              | WEB         | "Lord Yahweh" — preserves the Hebrew "Adonai Yahweh" with both titles intact, where KJV/ESV use "Lord GOD" (two English words for two different Hebrew words). NET: "O Sovereign Lord" — makes the authority aspect of "Adonai" explicit.
v.6              | NET         | "the Lord credited it as righteousness to him" — renders the foundational justification verse with "credited" (accounting language), emphasizing imputation. ASV: "reckoned it to him" — similarly formal.
v.12             | NET         | "great terror overwhelmed him" — more intense than "a horror of great darkness fell upon him" (KJV/ESV), emphasizing the dread.
v.16             | NET         | "the sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its limit" — interprets "iniquity...not yet full" with "reached its limit," making the divine patience/threshold concept more accessible.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
"I am thy shield and reward" | God as both protector (shield) and prize (reward). He is not just the giver but the gift itself. | 
Believed and counted for righteousness | Justification by faith. Abram trusted God's promise and God credited righteousness to his account. The foundation of salvation by grace through faith. | 
The covenant ceremony | Animals cut in half; parties walk between them. A self-maledictory oath: "May I be cut apart if I break this." | 
God alone passed through | The covenant is unconditional. God takes full responsibility. If it fails, God bears the consequences. | 
Smoking furnace and burning lamp | God's presence in the covenant ceremony. Fire and smoke — theophany.              | 
Four hundred years | The predicted duration of Israel's slavery in Egypt. Fulfilled in Exodus.         | 
From the river of Egypt to the Euphrates | The full boundaries of the Promised Land. God's gift to Abram's descendants.      | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward" (v.1). Is God Himself
   enough for you? Not just His gifts but HIM? He is both your protection
   and your prize.

2. "He believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness"
   (v.6). This is how you are saved — by believing God's promise. Not by
   works but by faith. Have you believed? Is His righteousness credited to
   your account?

3. God alone passed between the pieces (v.17). Your salvation depends on
   God's faithfulness, not yours. The covenant is unconditional — God took
   full responsibility. Does this give you security?

4. "Thy seed shall be a stranger... four hundred years" (v.13). God told
   Abram the future would include suffering BEFORE fulfillment. Are you in a
   "four hundred years" season — waiting, suffering, before the promise is
   fulfilled? God sees the whole timeline.

5. "The iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full" (v.16). God is patient
   even with the wicked — giving time for repentance. But there is a limit.
   How does God's patience with evil encourage and challenge you?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Genesis Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/genesis/

David Guzik — Genesis 15 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-15/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

16.ESV:
1. Now Sarai, Abram’s wife, had borne him no children. She had a female Egyptian servant whose name was Hagar.
2. And Sarai said to Abram, “Behold now, the LORD has prevented me from bearing children. Go in to my servant; it may be that I shall obtain children by her.” And Abram listened to the voice of Sarai.
3. So, after Abram had lived ten years in the land of Canaan, Sarai, Abram’s wife, took Hagar the Egyptian, her servant, and gave her to Abram her husband as a wife.
4. And he went in to Hagar, and she conceived. And when she saw that she had conceived, she looked with contempt on her mistress.
5. And Sarai said to Abram, “May the wrong done to me be on you! I gave my servant to your embrace, and when she saw that she had conceived, she looked on me with contempt. May the LORD judge between you and me!”
6. But Abram said to Sarai, “Behold, your servant is in your power; do to her as you please.” Then Sarai dealt harshly with her, and she fled from her.
7. The angel of the LORD found her by a spring of water in the wilderness, the spring on the way to Shur.
8. And he said, “Hagar, servant of Sarai, where have you come from and where are you going?” She said, “I am fleeing from my mistress Sarai.”
9. The angel of the LORD said to her, “Return to your mistress and submit to her.”
10. The angel of the LORD also said to her, “I will surely multiply your offspring so that they cannot be numbered for multitude.”
11. And the angel of the LORD said to her, “Behold, you are pregnant and shall bear a son. You shall call his name Ishmael, because the LORD has listened to your affliction.
12. He shall be a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone and everyone’s hand against him, and he shall dwell over against all his kinsmen.”
13. So she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, “You are a God of seeing,” for she said, “Truly here I have seen him who looks after me.”
14. Therefore the well was called Beer-lahai-roi; it lies between Kadesh and Bered.
15. And Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Ishmael.
16. Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.

16.notes:
Genesis - Chapter 16
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Sarai gives her servant Hagar to Abram; Hagar conceives Ishmael, but conflict erupts and God meets Hagar in the wilderness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Moses (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 16 records Abram and Sarai's attempt to fulfill
God's promise through human effort — taking Hagar (Sarai's Egyptian maid) as
a surrogate. This was culturally acceptable in the ancient Near East but
represented a failure of faith. The result: Ishmael — not the promised son
but a son of the flesh. The chapter also contains the first appearance of
"the angel of the LORD" (v.7) — likely a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ
(theophany) — who meets Hagar in her distress.

Sarai's Plan (vv.1-3): Sarai is barren (v.1). After 10 years in Canaan with
no child (v.3), she proposes Hagar as a surrogate: "go in unto my maid; it
may be that I may obtain children by her" (v.2). Abram agrees (v.2). This was
legal in ancient culture (cf. Code of Hammurabi) but not God's plan.

The Consequences (vv.4-6): Hagar conceives and despises Sarai (v.4). Sarai
blames Abram (v.5). Abram abdicates responsibility: "thy maid is in thy hand"
(v.6). Sarai mistreats Hagar; Hagar flees (v.6). A mess of human making.

The Angel of the LORD and Hagar (vv.7-14): The angel finds Hagar in the
wilderness (v.7). He asks: "whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go?"
(v.8). Commands: return and submit (v.9). Promises: innumerable descendants
(v.10). Names the child: Ishmael — "God hears" (v.11). Describes Ishmael:
"a wild man; his hand will be against every man" (v.12). Hagar names God:
"Thou God seest me" (v.13) — El Roi.

Ishmael's Birth (vv.15-16): Hagar returns. Ishmael born. Abram is 86 (v.16).

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
• Beer-lahai-roi (v.14) — meaning "the well of the Living One who sees me."
  Located between Kadesh and Bered in the Negev desert, along the road to
  Shur (toward Egypt).
• The wilderness of Shur (v.7) — the desert region between Canaan and Egypt,
  along the trade route. Hagar flees toward her homeland (Egypt).

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.7              | Exodus      | 3       | 2                  | "The angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire." (ESV)
v.11             | Matthew     | 1       | 21                 | "You shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins." (ESV)
v.13             | Psalm       | 139     | 7-12               | "Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence?" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/genesis-16/
  Guzik covers Sarai's plan, the consequences, the angel of the LORD, and
  God's care for Hagar.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The God Who Sees" on v.13 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1312)
  "Thou God SEEST me — El Roi. A runaway slave in the wilderness — alone,
  pregnant, desperate, outcast. And God SEES her. Not overlooks, not ignores,
  not forgets — sees. He sees the invisible ones. He notices the overlooked.
  He finds the lost. Hagar was nobody to the world — but God saw her, spoke
  to her, promised her a future. If God sees a runaway slave, He sees you.
  Wherever you are, however low, however forgotten — Thou God seest me."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|---------
v.2              | KJV         | "obtain children by her" / ESV: "obtain children by her" — Both identical. Hebrew "ibbaneh" — be built up through her. Surrogate motherhood was legal in ancient Mesopotamia. The child would legally be Sarai's. Culturally acceptable but not God's plan.
v.7              | KJV         | "the angel of the LORD" / ESV: "The angel of the LORD" — Both identical. Hebrew "malak Yahweh." First appearance of this figure. In many OT passages, the angel of the LORD speaks as God, is identified as God, yet is distinct from God — suggesting a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ.
v.11             | KJV         | "Ishmael" / ESV: "Ishmael" — Both identical. Hebrew "Yishma'el" — "God hears." KJV: "the LORD hath heard thy affliction" / ESV: "the LORD has listened to your affliction" — Both equivalent; ESV uses "listened" for Hebrew "shama."
v.12             | KJV         | "a wild man" / ESV: "a wild donkey of a man" — Hebrew "pere adam" — a wild donkey of a man. ESV translates more literally, including "donkey." The wild donkey was untameable, free-roaming, independent. Ishmael's descendants (Arab peoples) would be fierce, independent, and in conflict.
v.13             | KJV         | "Thou God seest me" / ESV: "You are a God of seeing" — Hebrew "El Roi" — the God of seeing, the God who sees me. Both capture the meaning; ESV emphasizes God's attribute of seeing, KJV emphasizes the personal experience of being seen. Hagar gives God a name based on her experience of being seen by Him.
v.14             | KJV         | "Beer-lahai-roi" / ESV: "Beer-lahai-roi" — Both identical. "The well of the Living One who sees me." A memorial to God's watchful care over the outcast. TRANSLATION NOTES (ASV, NET, WEB) ------------------------------------
v.2              | NET         | "please sleep with my servant" — renders Hebrew "bo el" (go in to) with modern directness where KJV/ASV use euphemistic "go in unto." NET also: "Perhaps I can have a family by her" — interprets "be built up" (Hebrew "ibaneh") as "have a family," preserving the wordplay on "banah" (build) and "ben" (son).
v.7              | WEB         | "Yahweh's angel" — preserves the construct form of the Hebrew. the outcast.
v.11             | NET         | "the Lord has heard your painful groans" — interprets "affliction" (KJV/ESV) as "painful groans," making Hagar's suffering more vivid and connecting it to the name Ishmael ("God hears").
v.12             | NET         | "He will be a wild donkey of a man" — vividly renders the Hebrew "pere adam" as an untameable figure of independence and conflict.
v.13             | WEB         | "You are a God who sees" — renders "El Roi" in accessible English. NET: "You are the God who sees me" — adds "me" for personal emphasis.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Hagar             | Sarai's Egyptian maid, given to Abram as a surrogate. Mother of Ishmael. An outcast whom God sees and cares for. | 
Ishmael           | "God hears." Abram's son through Hagar. Not the promised son but blessed by God. Ancestor of Arab peoples. Angel of the LORD — The first appearance of this figure. Likely a pre- incarnate Christ (theophany). Speaks as God, acts as God. | 
El Roi            | "The God who sees me." Hagar's name for God. He sees the invisible, the outcast, the forgotten. | 
Wild man          | Ishmael's prophetic character: fierce, independent, untameable, in conflict. Fulfilled in his descendants. Human effort to fulfill God's promise — Taking matters into your own hands rather than waiting for God's timing. Always produces complications. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Sarai's plan (v.2) was culturally acceptable but not God's plan. Where
   are you trying to fulfill God's promises through human effort rather than
   waiting for His timing? "Helping God" often creates more problems.

2. "The angel of the LORD found her" (v.7). God seeks the outcast, the
   runaway, the desperate. Have you ever felt invisible? God sees you. He
   finds you in the wilderness.

3. "Thou God seest me" (v.13). El Roi — the God who sees. He sees your pain,
   your situation, your need. You are not invisible to Him. Does this
   comfort you?

4. Ishmael was born from impatience — Abram was 86 and had waited 10 years
   (v.3). Isaac wouldn't come for 14 more years. God's timing often requires
   longer waiting than we expect. Can you wait?

5. The consequences of Sarai's plan continue to this day (Arab-Israeli
   conflict). Our impatient decisions can have generational consequences.
   What decisions are you making that might affect generations?
